mainmatter.p: Working in univalent foundations, let \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V}\) be a pair of not-necessarily-univalent universes such that \(\mathcal{U}\) is closed under \(\mathcal{V}\)-small products of \(\mathcal{U}\)-small types; then the subuniverse inclusion \({\mathcal{U}} \xrightarrow{j} {\mathcal{V}}\) is reflective. This argument will combine ideas due to Awodey, Frey, Speight, and Shulman. For any type \(A: \mathcal{V}\), we will define the reflection of \(A\) in \(\mathcal{U}\) in several steps.

Let \(\mathcal{U}\) be a subuniverse of \(\mathcal{V}\) and let \(A: \mathcal{V}\) be a type. A reflection algebra \(X\) under \(A\) in \(\mathcal{U}\) is defined to be a type \(UX: \mathcal{U}\) together with a function \({A} \xrightarrow{\eta _ {X}} {UX}\). A homomorphism \(f\) of reflection algebras from \(X\) to \(Y\) is given by a function \({UX} \xrightarrow{Uf} {UY}\) together with a homotopy \(\eta _ {f} : {\eta _ {Y}} \sim _ {A \to Y} {Uf \circ \eta _X}\).
When it causes no ambiguity, we will write \(X: \mathcal{U}\) for the underlying type \(UX\) of a given reflection algebra \(X\); likewise, we will write \(f x\) rather than \(Uf x\).

frontmatter.title: The Type of Reflection Algebras and Homomorphisms

mainmatter.p: We will write \({A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} : \equiv {\textstyle \sum _ {X: \mathcal{U}}} {A \to X}\) for the type of reflection algebras under \(A\); given two reflection algebras \(X,Y: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) we will write \({\left (X \multimap Y \right)} : \equiv {\textstyle \sum _ {f:X \to Y}} {\eta _ {Y}} \sim _ {A \to Y} {f \circ \eta _ {X}}\) for the type of homomorphisms of reflection algebras.

frontmatter.title: Identity and Composition of Reflection Algebra Homomorphisms

tree.mainmatter:
Let \(X: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) be a reflection algebra; we define the identity homomorphism \(1 _ {X} :X \multimap X\) on \(X\) as follows:\[ \begin{array} {l} 1 _ {X} x : \equiv x \\ \eta _ {1 _ {X}} a : \equiv \mathsf{refl} \end{array}\]
Now let \(Y,Z: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) be two further reflection algebras and let \(f : X \multimap Y\) and \(g : Y \multimap Z\) be two homomorphisms of reflection algebras. The composite homomorphism \(f;g : X \multimap Z\) is defined like so:\[ \begin{array} {l} {\left (f;g \right)} x : \equiv g {\left (fx \right)} \\ \eta _ {f;g} a : \equiv \mathsf{ap} _ {g} \, {\left (\eta _ {f} a \right)} \bullet \eta _ {g} a \end{array}\]

Given a pair of reflection algebras \(X,Y: {\mathcal{U}} \downarrow{A}\) and a pair of homomorphisms \(f,g : X \multimap  Y\), a homotopy from \(f\) to \(g\) is defined to be a homotopy \(\phi  :  {Uf}   \sim _ {X \to  Y}   {Ug}\) between the underlying functions together with a witness \(\bar{\phi}\)  of the following coherence:\[    \bar \phi  :
 {\textstyle \prod _ {a:A}} {   \eta _ {g} a
}  =_ {   {g {\left (\eta _ {X} a \right)}}  =_ {Y}   {\eta _ {Y} a}}   {   \eta _ {f} a \bullet   \phi {\left (\eta _ {X} a \right)}}\]
We shall write \({f}   \sim _ {X \multimap  Y}   {g}\) for the type of homotopies from the homomorphism \(f\) to the homomorphism \(g\).

mainmatter.p: Given a path of reflection algebras \(X,Y: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) and a homomorphism \(f : X \multimap Y\), the type \({\textstyle \sum _ {g:X \multimap Y}} {{g} \sim _ {X \multimap Y} {f}}\) of homomorphisms equipped with a homotopy to \(f\) is contractible, by function extensionality and the characterization of path spaces in dependent sums. Hence it follows that the canonical map \({f} =_ {X \multimap Y} {g} \to {f} \sim _ {X \multimap Y} {g}\) is an equivalence.

tree.mainmatter: Given a type \(K\) and a family of reflection algebras \(X : K \to {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) such that the dependent product \({\textstyle \prod _ {k:K}} U {\left (Xk \right)}\) lies in \(\mathcal{U}\), we may define the product of product of reflection algebras \({\textstyle \prod _ {k:K}} Xk\) to have the following structure map:\[ \begin{array} {l} \eta _ {{\textstyle \prod _ {k:K}} Xk} : A \to {\textstyle \prod _ {k:K}} {Xk} \\ \eta _ {{\textstyle \prod _ {k:K}} Xk} a : \equiv \lambda k:K. \, \eta _ {Xk} a \end{array}\]

mainmatter.p: Let \(X: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) be a reflection algebra; a displayed reflection algebra \(Y\) over \(X\) is defined to be a dependent type \(\bar{U} Y : X \to \mathcal{U}\) together with a dependent function \(\bar{\eta} _ {Y} : {\textstyle \prod _ {a:A}} {\bar{U} Y {\left (\eta _ {X} a \right)}} .\)
We will write \(Y [x]\) for \(\bar{U} Yx\).

tree.mainmatter:
Given a displayed reflection algebra \(Y\) over a reflection algebra \(X: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\), we may define the total reflection algebra \(\widetilde{Y} : {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) of \(Y\) as follows:\[ \begin{array} {l} U \widetilde{Y} : \equiv {\textstyle \sum _ {x:Ux}} Y [x] \\ \eta _ {\widetilde{Y}} a : \equiv {\left (\eta _ {X} a, \bar{\eta} _ {Y} a \right)} \end{array}\]
The projection function \({U \widetilde{Y}} \xrightarrow{\pi _1} {UX}\) extends to a homomorphism of reflection algebras \(\pi _ {Y} : \widetilde{Y} \multimap X\) as follows:\[ \begin{array} {l} \pi _ {Y} {\left (x,y \right)} : \equiv x \\ \alpha _ {\pi _ {Y}} a : \equiv \mathsf{refl} \end{array}\]

frontmatter.title: Displayed Reflection Algebras Over Displayed Reflection Algebras

mainmatter.p: If \(Y\) is a displayed reflection algebra over a reflection algebra \(X: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\), we define a displayed reflection algebra over \(Y\) to be a displayed reflection algebra in the original sense over the total reflection algebra \(\widetilde{Y}\) corresponding to \(Y\).

Letting \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V}\) be an impredicative pair of universes with \(A: \mathcal{V}\) a type in the outer universe, our goal is to construct a limit cone over the identity functor in the (∞,1)-category \({A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) of reflection algebras in \(\mathcal{U}\) under \(A: \mathcal{V}\), in order to prove that there is an initial reflection algebra. Even though we do not have access to the fully coherent language of (∞,1)-category theory in univalent foundations, we can specify the coherence structure that we need in order to obtain our result.

Our first step is describe a classifying reflection algebra for fully incoherent cones over the identity functor on \({A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\), which we do by means of a displayed reflection algebra of incoherent naturality structures on the product of all reflection algebras.

tree.mainmatter:
Let \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V}\) be an impredicative universe pair with \(A: \mathcal{V}\) a type in the outer universe, so that we may form the product \({\textstyle \prod _ {X: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}}} X\) of all reflection algebras in \(\mathcal{U}\) under \(A\). To express the data of the components of a given \(u: {\textstyle \prod _ {X: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}}} X\) being preserved by all homomorphisms and thus representing “cones” over the identity functor on \({A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\), we define a displayed reflection algebra \(\mathsf{INatStr}\) of “incoherent naturality structures” over \({\textstyle \prod _ {X: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}}} {X}\) as follows:\[ \begin{array} {l} \mathsf{INatStr} [u] : \equiv {\textstyle \prod _ {{\left \{ X,Y: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} \right \}}}} {\textstyle \prod _ {f : X \multimap Y}} {f {\left (uX \right)}} =_ {Y} {uY} \\ \bar{\eta} _ {\mathsf{INatStr}} \, a \, f : \equiv \eta _ {f} a \end{array}\]
Given an element \(u: \widetilde{\mathsf{INatStr}}\), we will write \(\epsilon _ {u} : {\textstyle \prod _ {X: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}}} X\) for the first component \(\pi _ {\mathsf{INatStr}} u\) and \(\vartheta _ {u} : \mathsf{INatStr} [\epsilon _ {u}]\) for the second component.
This definition is equivalent to one given by Awodey, Frey, and Speight.

mainmatter.p: As suggested by the name, the data of an incoherent naturality structure over a family \(u: {\textstyle \prod _ {X: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}}} UX\) is not particularly coherent. It will happen that for our goals, we need only add a single dimension of additional coherence data governing the behavior of the naturality structure on binary compositions of homomorphisms. This will give us a classifier for what we shall refer to as “semicoherent” cones over the identity functor on \({A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\).

tree.mainmatter:
Let \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V}\) be an impredicative universe pair with \(A: \mathcal{V}\) such that \(\mathsf{INatStr}\) is the displayed reflection algebra of incoherent naturality structures over the product \({\textstyle \prod _ {X: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}}} X\) of all reflection algebras. We define a further displayed reflection algebra \(\mathsf{SemiCoh}\) over \(\mathsf{INatStr}\) of “semicoherence structures” associating to each incoherent naturality structure one dimension of coherence data for binary compositions of homomorphisms.
In particular, we define \(\mathsf{SemiCoh}\) over \(\widetilde{\mathsf{INatStr}}\) as follows, employing our notion of homotopy between homomorphisms:\[ \begin{array} {l} \mathsf{SemiCoh} [u] : \equiv \\ \begin{array} {l} {\textstyle \prod _ {{\left \{ X,Y,Z: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} \right \}}}} {\textstyle \prod _ {f : X \multimap Y}} {\textstyle \prod _ {g : Y \multimap Z}} {\textstyle \prod _ {h : X \multimap Z}} {\textstyle \prod _ {\phi : {f;g} \sim _ {X \multimap Z} {h}}} \\ \mathsf{ap} _ {g} {\left (\vartheta _ {u} f \right)} \bullet \vartheta _ {u} g = \phi {\left (\epsilon _ {u} X \right)} \bullet \vartheta _ {u} h \end{array} \end{array}\]
To define the displayed algebra structure \(\bar \eta _ {\mathsf{SemiCoh}}\), we will use the contractibility of singletons several times. In particular, given \({\left (Y,f \right)}\) where \(Y: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) and \(f:X \multimap Y\), the algebra component \(\eta _ {Y}\) is completely determined by the homotopy \(\eta _ {f} : {\eta _ {Y}} \sim _ {A \to Y} {f \circ \eta _ {X}}\); likewise, by our characterization of paths between homomorphisms, the pair \({\left (h, \phi \right)}\) is uniquely determined. Therefore, we may define \(\bar \eta _ {\mathsf{SemiCoh}}\) to be the unique function satisfying the following equation:\[\bar \eta _ {\mathsf{SemiCoh}} \, a \, {\left (f, \lambda \_ . \mathsf{refl} \right)} \, {\left (g, \lambda \_ . \mathsf{refl} \right)} \, {\left ({\left (f;g \right)} , \lambda \_ . \mathsf{refl} \right)} , {\left (\lambda \_ . \mathsf{refl} , \lambda \_ . \mathsf{refl} \right)} := \mathsf{refl}\]
Given an element \(u: \widetilde{\mathsf{SemiCoh}}\), we shall write \(\epsilon _ {u} , \vartheta _ {u}\) for the components of its underlying incoherent naturality structure \(\pi _ {\mathsf{SemiCoh}} u\) and \(\varsigma _ {u} : \mathsf{SemiCoh} [\pi _ {\mathsf{SemiCoh}} u]\) for the semicoherence structure itself.
This definition is similar to that of Awodey, Frey, and Speight, except that we do not impose any coherence law for identities.

By the contractibility of singletons and our characterization of paths between homomorphisms, the definition of semicoherence structures is equivalent to following more direct formulation that avoids the additional cell \(\phi\):\[ {\textstyle \prod _ {{\left \{ X,Y,Z: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} \right \}}}} {\textstyle \prod _ {f : X \multimap Y}} {\textstyle \prod _ {g : Y \multimap Z}} \mathsf{ap} _ {g} {\left (\vartheta _ {u} f \right)} \bullet \vartheta _ {u} g = \vartheta _ {u} {\left (f;g \right)}\]
Our reason for choosing the more complex definition involving \({\left (h, \phi \right)}\) is that the extra flexibility comes in handy in our results, as suggested by Michael Shulman in private communication.

frontmatter.title: A Generic Cone Over the Identity Functor and its Generic Element

tree.mainmatter:
Letting \(I : \equiv \widetilde{\mathsf{SemiCoh}}\) be the total algebra of the displayed reflection algebra of semicoherence structures, we may define a dependent function\[ p _ {\bullet} : {\textstyle \prod _ {X: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}}} I \multimap X \]
exhibiting a “incoherent cone” from \(I\) onto the identity functor on \({A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\). In particular, we define the components of \(p _ {\bullet}\) as follows:\[ \begin{array} {l} p _ {X} \, u : \equiv \epsilon _ {u} X \\ \eta _ {p _ {X}} a : \equiv \mathsf{refl} \end{array}\]
Above we have used the definition of the structure map on the total reflection algebra as well as that of the displayed structure map on \(\mathsf{SemiCoh}\) over \(\mathsf{INatStr}\) over \({\textstyle \prod _ {X: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}}} X\). We shall refer to \(p _ {\bullet}\) as the generic cone over the identity functor, with the caveat that we have not formally established it as a “cone” of any kind. Furthermore, we shall refer to the component \(p _ {I} :I \multimap I\) as the generic element of the generic cone.

frontmatter.title: Quasi-idempotence of the Generic Element of the Generic Cone

tree.mainmatter:
The generic element \(p _ {I} :I \multimap I\) of the generic cone \(p _ {\bullet} : {\textstyle \prod _ {X: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}}} I \multimap X\) under \(I \equiv \widetilde{\mathsf{SemiCoh}}\) can be equipped with the structure of a pre-idempotent. In particular, we may define a homotopy \(\chi : {p _ {I} ;p _ {I}} \sim _ {I \multimap I} {p _ {I}}\) using the incoherent naturality structure of any element of \(I\).\[ \begin{array} {l} \chi \, u : \equiv \vartheta _ {u} p _ {I} \\ \bar{\chi} a : \equiv \mathsf{refl} \end{array}\]
That the higher identification \(\bar \chi{a} : \eta _ {p _ {I}} a = \eta _ {p _ {I} ;p _ {I}} \bullet \vartheta _ {\eta _ {I} a} p _ {I}\) may be defined by reflexivity follows from the definition of \(\eta _ {I} a\), the computation of composite homomorphisms, and the fact that \(\eta _ {p _ {I}} a\) is also defined by reflexivity; in particular, we compute:\[ \begin{aligned} \eta _ {p _ {I} ;p _ {I}} \bullet \vartheta _ {\eta _ {I} a} p _ {I} & \equiv {\left (\mathsf{ap} _ {p _ {I}} {\left (\eta _ {p _ {I}} a \right)} \bullet \eta _ {p _ {I}} a \right)} \bullet \eta _ {p _ {I}} a \\ & \equiv {\left (\mathsf{refl} \bullet \mathsf{refl} \right)} \bullet \mathsf{refl} \\ & \equiv \mathsf{refl} \bullet \mathsf{refl} \\ & \equiv \mathsf{refl} \end{aligned}\]
We will use the semicoherence structure associated to any element of \(I\) to exhibit the underlying function \(p _ {I} :I \to I\) of the generic element as a quasi-idempotent in the sense of Shulman. In particular, we must exhibit for each \(u:I\) an identification \(\psi : \mathsf{ap} _ {p _ {I}} {\left (\chi u \right)} = \chi {\left (p _ {I} u \right)}\). Unfolding definitions, this means to identify \(\mathsf{ap} _ {p _ {I}} {\left (\vartheta _ {u} p _ {I} \right)}\) with \(\vartheta _ {p _ {I} u} p _ {I}\).
From the semicoherence structure \(\varsigma _ {u} : \mathsf{SemiCoh} [\pi _ {\mathsf{SemiCoh}} u]\) we obtain the following identification:\[ \varsigma _ {u} \, p _ {I} \, p _ {I} \, p _ {I} \, \chi : \mathsf{ap} _ {p _ {I}} {\left (\vartheta _ {u} p _ {I} \right)} \bullet \vartheta _ {u} p _ {I} = \chi {\left (\epsilon _ {u} I \right)} \bullet \vartheta _ {u} p _ {I}\]
Canceling \(\vartheta _ {u} p _ {I}\), unfolding the definition of \(\chi\), and recalling that \(p _ {I} u \equiv \epsilon _ {u} I\) by definition, we obtain the desired identification \(\mathsf{ap} _ {p _ {I}} {\left (\vartheta _ {u} p _ {I} \right)} = \vartheta _ {p _ {I} u} p _ {I}\).
Therefore, the underlying function \(p _ {I} :I \to I\) has the structure of a quasi-idempotent.

frontmatter.title: Splitting the Generic Element of the Generic Cone

tree.mainmatter:
The underlying function \(p _ {I} :I \to I\) of the generic element \(p _ {I} :I \multimap I\) of the generic cone \(p _ {\bullet} : {\textstyle \prod _ {X: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}}} I \multimap X\) under \(I \equiv \widetilde{\mathsf{SemiCoh}}\) has been equipped with the structure of a quasi-idempotent in the sense of Shulman, so we may split it.
In particular, the construction of Shulman gives us a type \(J\) together with a retraction \(r : I \to J\) and a section \(s : J \to I\) witnessed by a homotopy \(\rho : {s;r} \sim _ {J \to J} {1 _ {J}}\), such that \(r;s \equiv p _ {I} : I \to I\) definitionally. Our goal is to lift this splitting of the underlying function \(p _ {I} :I \to I\) to a splitting of the homomorphism \(p _ {I} :I \multimap I\).
We may equip \(J\) with the structure of a reflection algebra by setting \(\eta _ {J} : \equiv \eta _ {I} ;r\), making \(r : I \multimap J\) a homomorphism of algebras definitionally, i.e. \(\eta _ {r} a: \equiv \mathsf{refl}\). To exhibit \(s : J \multimap I\) as a homomorphism of algebras, we may likewise use reflexivity:\[ \begin{array} {l} \eta _ {s} : {\eta _ {J} ;s} \sim _ {A \to I} {\eta _ {I}} \\ \eta _ {s} a : \equiv \mathsf{refl} \end{array}\]
That the above is well-defined can be deduced as follows, using the fact that the splitting of \(p _ {I} \equiv r;s\) is definitional and that \(p _ {I}\) is a definitional homomorphism:\[ \begin{aligned} s {\left (\eta _ {J} a \right)} & \equiv s {\left (r {\left (\eta _ {I} a \right)} \right)} \\ & \equiv p _ {I} {\left (\eta _ {I} a \right)} \\ & \equiv \eta _ {I} a \end{aligned}\]
We have to check that the homotopy of functions \(\rho : {s;r} \sim _ {J \to J} {1 _ {J}}\) lifts to a homotopy of homomorphisms. In particular, we must construct the coherence \(\bar \rho\) specified below:\[ \bar \rho : {\textstyle \prod _ {a : A}} \eta _ {1 _ {J}} a = \eta _ {s;r} a \bullet \rho {\left (\eta _ {J} a \right)}\]
Note that \(\eta _ {1 _ {J}}\) is reflexivity; we compute the other side of the desired identificiation \(\bar \rho a\) by unfolding definitions (e.g. that of identities and compositions of homomorphisms) and using basic path algebra:\[ \begin{aligned} \eta _ {s;r} a \bullet \rho {\left (\eta _ {J} a \right)} & \equiv {\left (\mathsf{ap} _ {r} {\left (\eta _ {s} a \right)} \bullet \eta _ {r} a \right)} \bullet \rho {\left (r {\left (\eta _ {I} a \right)} \right)} \\ & \equiv {\left (\mathsf{ap} _ {r} \mathsf{refl} \bullet \mathsf{refl} \right)} \bullet \rho {\left (r {\left (\eta _ {I} a \right)} \right)} \\ & \equiv {\left (\mathsf{refl} \bullet \mathsf{refl} \right)} \bullet \rho {\left (r {\left (\eta _ {I} a \right)} \right)} \\ & \equiv \mathsf{refl} \bullet \rho {\left (r {\left (\eta _ {I} a \right)} \right)} \\ & = \rho {\left (r {\left (\eta _ {I} a \right)} \right)} \end{aligned}\]
Therefore, to construct \(\bar \rho a\) is suffices to identify \(\rho {\left (r {\left (\eta _ {I} a \right)} \right)}\) with reflexivity. To do so, we will use an argument suggested by Shulman in private conversation. We recall from Theorem 3.3 of Shulman that the homotopy \(\rho\) induces a new witness of pre-idempotency for \(p _ {I}\), namely \(\chi ' x : \equiv \mathsf{ap} _ {s} {\left (\rho {\left (rx \right)} \right)}\) and that by Theorem 7.1 of the same, this homotopy may be identified with our original witness \(\chi : {p _ {I} ;p _ {I}} \sim _ {I \to I} {p _ {I}}\). Therefore we have \(\mathsf{ap} _ {s} {\left (\rho {\left (rx \right)} \right)} = \vartheta _ {x} p _ {I}\); because \(s\) is a section of \(r\), this implies that \(\rho {\left (r x \right)} = \mathsf{ap} _ {r} {\left (\vartheta _ {x} p _ {I} \right)}\) and so we may compute our identification \(\bar \rho \, a\) as follows:\[ \begin{aligned} \rho {\left (r {\left (\eta _ {I} a \right)} \right)} & = \mathsf{ap} _ {r} {\left (\vartheta _ {\eta _ {I} a} p _ {I} \right)} \\ & \equiv \mathsf{ap} _ {r} {\left (\eta _ {p _ {I}} a \right)} \\ & \equiv \mathsf{ap} _ {r} \mathsf{refl} \\ & \equiv \mathsf{refl} \end{aligned}\]
Above we have used the fact that the coherence datum \(\eta _ {p _ {I}} a\) is reflexivity.

mainmatter.p: Our main theorem then follows from wild categorical considerations, as we show below.

frontmatter.title: The Main Result: Reflectivity of Impredicative Subuniverses

mainmatter.p: Let \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V}\) be an impredicative pair of universes with \(A: \mathcal{V}\). There we may construct a reflection algebra \(A _ {\mathcal{U}} : {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) such that for any other reflection algebra \(X: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\), the space of homomorphisms \(A _ {\mathcal{U}} \multimap X\) is contractible.

details.p: Our splitting of the generic element of the generic cone \({\Delta I} \xrightarrow{p} {1 _ {{A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}}}\) over the identity functor on reflection algebras yields a new (incoherent) cone \({\Delta J} \xrightarrow{q} {1 _ {{A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}}}\) whose generic element \(q _ {J} :J \multimap J\) may be identified with the identity homomorphism. It follows that each space of homomorphisms \(J \to X\) is contractible. Therefore, we define \(A _ {\mathcal{U}}\) to be the apex \(J\).

mainmatter.p: If \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V}\) is an impredicative pair of universes, then \(\mathcal{U}\) is reflective in \(\mathcal{V}\).

details.p: Our construction of the initial reflection algebra for each \(A: \mathcal{V}\) is one of several equivalent formulations of reflective subuniverse in univalent foundations.

mainmatter.p: On this website, I organize my thoughts on a variety of topics at a granular level. Sometimes these thoughts are self-contained, and at times I may organize them into larger notebooks or lecture notes. My nascent ideas about the design of tools for mathematical thought are here. I welcome collaboration on any of the topics represented in my forest.

I am accumulating an understanding of constructive domain theory — that is, the domain theory that can be carried out in an elementary topos with a natural numbers object. These notes are based on the work of Tom de Jong and Martín Escardó; these notes, however, assume the propositional resizing principle whereas de Jong and Escardó explore a predicative version domain theory based on large and locally small domains.

In this section, we gather some useful definitions and results concerning creation of (co)limits. The reason for stating these things explicitly is that the usual definitions of created (co)limits are deeply irrational in a variety of incompatible ways, so it is important to fix definitions that can work from the start. It would be good to reformulate this in terms of displayed categories of algebras.

Let \({\mathcal{C}} \xrightarrow{U} {\mathcal{D}}\) be a functor and let \(\mathcal{I}\) be a category. The functor \(U\) is said to create (co)limits of \(\mathcal{I}\)-figures when for any diagram \({\mathcal{I}} \xrightarrow{C_ \bullet} {\mathcal{C}}\) such that \(\mathcal{I} \xrightarrow{C_ \bullet} \mathcal{C} \xrightarrow{F} \mathcal{D}\) has a (co)limit, then \(C_ \bullet\) has a (co)limit that is both preserved and reflected by \(F\).
We aim to understand sufficient conditions for a forgetful functor to create colimits. As we will show below, it is sufficient for these colimits to be preserved the the monad.

frontmatter.title: Algebra Structure on the Colimit Apex

mainmatter.p: Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a category and let \(\mathbb{T} = {\left (T, \eta , \mu \right)}\) be a monad on \(\mathcal{C}\), writing \({\mathcal{C} ^ {\mathbb{T}}} \xrightarrow{U} {\mathcal{C}}\) for the forgetful functor from the Eilenberg-Moore category \(\mathcal{C} ^ {\mathbb{T}}\). Suppose that \(T\) preserves colimits of \(\mathcal{I}\)-figures for a given small category \(\mathcal{I}\). Let \({\mathcal{I}} \xrightarrow{X_ \bullet} {\mathcal{C} ^ {\mathbb{T}}}\) be a diagram in \(\mathcal{C} ^ {\mathbb{T}}\) such that \(\mathcal{I} \xrightarrow{X_ \bullet} \mathcal{C} ^ {\mathbb{T}} \xrightarrow{U} \mathcal{C}\) has a universal cocone \({UX_ \bullet} \xrightarrow{c_ \bullet} {{\left \{ C \right \}}}\). We may lift \(C\) to an essentially unique \(\mathbb{T}\)-algebra structure \(\bar{C}\) with \(U \bar{C} =C\) in a canonical way such that \({UX_ \bullet} \xrightarrow{c_ \bullet} {{\left \{ C \right \}}}\) lifts to a cocone of algebras \({X _ {\bullet}} \xrightarrow{\bar{c} _ {\bullet}} {\bar{C}}\) with \(U \bar{c} _ \bullet =c_ \bullet\).

details.p: By assumption, \({TUX_ \bullet} \xrightarrow{Tc_ \bullet} {{\left \{ TC \right \}}}\) is a universal cocone. We may define a further cocone \(TUX_ \bullet \xrightarrow{\alpha _ {X_ \bullet}} UX_ \bullet \xrightarrow{c_ \bullet} {\left \{ C \right \}}\). The universal property of \(Tc_ \bullet\) then gives us a unique morphism \({TC} \xrightarrow{\beta} {C}\) satisfying the following condition:
We must show that the map \({TC} \xrightarrow{\beta} {C}\) depicted above satisfies the axioms of a \(\mathbb{T}\)-algebra.

li.p: To show that \(C \xrightarrow{\eta _C} TC \xrightarrow{\beta} C\) is the identity, we note that \({UX_ \bullet} \xrightarrow{c_ \bullet} {{\left \{ C \right \}}}\) is universal so it suffices to check that \(UX_ \bullet \xrightarrow{c_ \bullet} {\left \{ C \right \}} \xrightarrow{{\left \{ \eta _C \right \}}} {\left \{ TC \right \}} \xrightarrow{{\left \{ \beta \right \}}} {{\left \{ C \right \}}}\) is equal to \(c_ \bullet\) by the uniqueness property. This follows by inspection of the following commuting diagram, in which the top square is the naturality of the unit:

ol.li:
We must check that the the following multiplication square commutes:
By assumption, we know that \({TTUX_ \bullet} \xrightarrow{TTc_ \bullet} {{\left \{ TTC \right \}}}\) is a universal cocone; using this fact as well as the naturality of \(\mu\), it suffices to check that the following diagram commutes:
We compute algebraically, using the algebra property of \(X\) among other assumptions: \[ \begin{aligned} TTc_ \bullet ; {\left \{ T \beta \right \}} ; {\left \{ \beta \right \}} & = T {\left (Tc_ \bullet ; {\left \{ \beta \right \}} \right)} ; {\left \{ \beta \right \}} \\ & = T {\left (\alpha _ {X_ \bullet} ;c_ \bullet \right)} ; {\left \{ \beta \right \}} \\ & = T \alpha _ {X_ \bullet} ;Tc_ \bullet ; {\left \{ \beta \right \}} \\ & = T \alpha _ {X_ \bullet} ; \alpha _ {X_ \bullet} ;c_ \bullet \\ & = \mu _ {UX_ \bullet} ; \alpha _ {X_ \bullet} ; c_ \bullet \\ & = \mu _ {UX_ \bullet} ;Tc_ \bullet ; {\left \{ \beta \right \}} \end{aligned} \]
Hence we may define a \(\mathbb{T}\)-algebra structure \(\bar{C}\) with \(U \bar{C} =P\), setting \({TC} \xrightarrow{\alpha _ {\bar{C}}} {C}\) to be \(\beta\). That \(c_ \bullet\) lifts to a cocone of algebras is exactly the defining condition of \(\alpha _ {\bar{C}} = \beta\) via the universal property of \({TUX_ \bullet} \xrightarrow{Tc_ \bullet} {{\left \{ TC \right \}}}\); uniqueness of the algebra structure with this property follows from the same universal property.

frontmatter.title: Reflection of Colimits by the Forgetful Functor

mainmatter.p: Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a category and let \(\mathbb{T} = {\left (T, \eta , \mu \right)}\) be a monad on \(\mathcal{C}\), writing \({\mathcal{C} ^ {\mathbb{T}}} \xrightarrow{U} {\mathcal{C}}\) for the forgetful functor from the Eilenberg-Moore category \(\mathcal{C} ^ {\mathbb{T}}\). If \(T\) preserves colimits of \(\mathcal{I}\)-figures for a given indexing category \(\mathcal{I}\), then \({\mathcal{C} ^ {\mathbb{T}}} \xrightarrow{U} {\mathcal{C}}\) reflects colimits of \(\mathcal{I}\)-figures.

section.details:
Proof.
Let \({\mathcal{I}} \xrightarrow{X_ \bullet} {\mathcal{C} ^ {\mathbb{T}}}\) be a diagram in \(\mathcal{C} ^ {\mathbb{T}}\) and let \({X_ \bullet} \xrightarrow{y_ \bullet} {{\left \{ Y \right \}}}\) be a cocone in \(\mathcal{C} ^ {\mathbb{T}}\) such that \({UX_ \bullet} \xrightarrow{Uy_ \bullet} {{\left \{ UY \right \}}}\) is a universal cocone for \(UX_ \bullet = \mathcal{I} \xrightarrow{X_ \bullet} \mathcal{C} ^ {\mathbb{T}} \xrightarrow{U} \mathcal{C}\). We must show that \({X_ \bullet} \xrightarrow{y_ \bullet} {{\left \{ Y \right \}}}\) is a universal cocone.
In particular, we must factor any cocone \({X_ \bullet} \xrightarrow{z_ \bullet} {{\left \{ Z \right \}}}\) uniquely through \({X_ \bullet} \xrightarrow{y_ \bullet} {{\left \{ Y \right \}}}\). Because \(Uy_ \bullet\) is universal, there exists a unique \({Y} \xrightarrow{h} {Z}\) such that the following diagram commutes:
We must check that \(h\) is a homomorphism of \(\mathbb{T}\)-algebras:
To see that \(\alpha _Y;h = Th; \alpha _Z\) it suffices to observe that they both factor the cocone \[TUX_ \bullet \xrightarrow{\alpha _ {X_ \bullet}} UX_ \bullet \xrightarrow{Uz_ \bullet} {\left \{ UZ \right \}}\]
through the cocone \({TUX_ \bullet} \xrightarrow{TUy_ \bullet} {{\left \{ TUY \right \}}}\), which is universal by our assumption that \(T\) preserves \(\mathcal{I}\)-colimits.
First we must check that the following diagram commutes:
This holds immediately from the homomorphism property of \(y_ \bullet\).
Second, we must check that the following diagram commutes:
This follows from the factorization \(Uy_ \bullet ;h=Uz_ \bullet\) and the homomorphism property of \(z_ \bullet\).

frontmatter.title: Creation of Colimits by the Forgetful Functor

mainmatter.p: Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a category and let \(\mathbb{T} = {\left (T, \eta , \mu \right)}\) be a monad on \(\mathcal{C}\), writing \({\mathcal{C} ^ {\mathbb{T}}} \xrightarrow{U} {\mathcal{C}}\) for the forgetful functor from the Eilenberg-Moore category \(\mathcal{C} ^ {\mathbb{T}}\). If \(T\) preserves colimits of \(\mathcal{I}\)-figures for a given small category \(\mathcal{I}\), then \({\mathcal{C} ^ {\mathbb{T}}} \xrightarrow{U} {\mathcal{C}}\) creates colimits of \(\mathcal{I}\)-figures.

details.p: Let \({\mathcal{I}} \xrightarrow{X_ \bullet} {\mathcal{C} ^ {\mathbb{T}}}\) be a diagram such that \(\mathcal{I} \xrightarrow{X_ \bullet} \mathcal{C} ^ {\mathbb{T}} \xrightarrow{U} \mathcal{C}\) has a (co)limit cocone \({UX_ \bullet} \xrightarrow{c_ \bullet} {{\left \{ C \right \}}}\). Let \(\bar{C} = {\left (C, \alpha _ {\bar{C}} \right)}\) be the canonical algebra structure on the colimit apex \(C\) such that \({UX_ \bullet} \xrightarrow{c_ \bullet} {{\left \{ C \right \}}}\) lifts to a cocone of algebras. Then we conclude that this cocone is universal because \(U\) reflects colimits of \(\mathcal{I}\)-figures. It is evident (by definition) that \(U\) preserves this colimit.

mainmatter.p: A dcpo is called pointed when it has a bottom element \(\bot\) under the information order.

frontmatter.title: Suprema of Semidirected Subsets in Pointed Dcpos

mainmatter.p: A dcpo \(A\) is pointed if and only if it is closed under suprema of semidirected subsets.

details.p: Suppose that \(A\) is closed under suprema of semidirected subsets. In particular, we may consider the supremum \(\bigsqcup {\left \{ \right \}}\) of the empty set. This is evidently the bottom element, using the universal property of the supremum.

Conversely, suppose that \(A\) is pointed and let \(I \subseteq A\) be semidirected. We may replace \(I \subseteq A\) by the directed subset \(I' = I \cup {\left \{ \bot \right \}}\); the inclusion \(I \subseteq I'\) is clearly cofinal, so the supremum of \(I'\) is also the supremum of \(I\).

mainmatter.p: A pointed dcpo carries a (unique) \(\mathbb{L}\)-algebra structure.

section.details:
Proof.
The algebra structure is unique if it exists because \(\mathbb{L}\) is a Kock–Zöberlein monad, i.e. a monad for which the structure maps of algebras are left adjoint to the unit in the order-enrichment. Therefore, it suffices to show that there merely exists an algebra structure \({\mathsf{L} {A}} \xrightarrow{\alpha} {A}\) for any pointed dcpo \(A\).
We define the structure map \(\alpha\) to take \(u \in \mathsf{L} {A}\) to the supremum of the semidirected subset \({\left \{ x \in A \mid u = \eta _Ax \right \}}\), so we may write \(\alpha u = \bigsqcup _ {p:u {\downarrow}} u [p]\). It is not difficult to see that this assignment is continuous.
The unit law for the algebra is trivial. The multiplication law is verified as follows: \[\alpha {\left (\mathsf{L} {\alpha} {\left (\phi ,u \right)} \right)} = \alpha {\left (\phi , \alpha u \right)} = \bigsqcup _ {p:u {\downarrow}} \bigsqcup _ {q:u [p] {\downarrow}} u [p] [q] = \alpha {\left (\mu _A {\left (\phi ,u \right)} \right)} \]

mainmatter.p: Let \({\left (A, \alpha \right)}\) be a \(\mathbb{L}\)-algebra; then the underlying dcpo \(A\) is pointed.

details.p: To define the bottom element of \(A\), we choose \(\alpha {\left (\bot , \lambda \_ . \star \right)}\). Fixing \(n \in A\), we must check that \(\alpha {\left (\bot , \lambda \_ . \star \right)} \sqsubseteq n\). Because \(\mathsf{L}\) is a Kock–Zöberlein monad and thus we have \(\alpha \dashv \eta _ {A}\), it is equivalent to observe that \({\left (\bot , \lambda \_ . \star \right)} \sqsubseteq \eta _ {A} n\).

mainmatter.p: Let \({\left (A, \alpha \right)}\) and \({\left (B, \beta \right)}\) be two \(\mathbb{L}\)-algebras; then a morphism of dcpos \({A} \xrightarrow{f} {B}\) is a homomorphism of algebras if and only if it preserves the bottom element.

section.details:
Proof.
It is clear that a homomorphism of algebras must preserve the bottom element. On the other hand, suppose that \({A} \xrightarrow{f} {B}\) preserves the bottom element to check that the following diagram commutes:
Because both composites preserve the bottom element, it is enough to observe that they both take \(u \in TA\) to the supremum of the following semidirected subset: \[{\left \{ n \in B \mid \exists m:A. u= \eta _ {A} m \land n=fm \right \}}\]

mainmatter.p: The category of \(\mathbb{L}\)-algebras and homomorphisms is (canonically) equivalent to the category of pointed dcpos and bottom-preserving morphisms of dcpos. It follows that pointed dcpos are monadic over dcpos.

details.p: Having a bottom element and being an algebra are both properties of dcpos, because these structures are uniquely determined. Therefore, we will argue that pointed dcpos with bottom-preserving morphisms form the same subcategory of \(\mathsf{dcpo}\) as the category of algebras with algebra-preserving maps. We have already seen that pointed structure and algebra structure are interchangeable:
To finish, we recall that a morphism of dcpos preserves algebra structure if and only if it preserves the bottom element.

mainmatter.p: By virtue of the monadicity result, we will refer to pointed dcpos and \(\mathbb{L}\)-algebras interchangeably.

We will deduce the closure of pointed dcpos under connected colimits using general results about monads. If we can show the closure of lifted dcpos under coproducts, we will have established the cocompleteness of the category of pointed dcpos and bottom-preserving maps.
For any dcpo \(A\), the following is a co-comma square in the posetal 2-category of dcpos, where \(\mathsf{L}\) takes a dcpo to its lift or Scott-open partial map classifier.

section.details:
Proof.
We fix another lax square of the following form:
We must show that there exists a unique morphism \(\mathsf{L} {A} \xrightarrow{d} D\) factoring the square above through \({\left \{ d_ \bot \sqsubseteq d_A \right \}}\) as follows:
We will define \(\mathsf{L} {A} \xrightarrow{d} D\) to take \(a ^ {?} : \mathsf{L} {A}\) to the least upper bound of the directed subset \(U = {\left \{ d_ \bot \right \}} \cup {\left \{ d_A a \mid a ^ {?} = \eta _Aa \right \}} \subseteq D\). As this assignment evidently has the correct factorization property, it remains just to check that it is continuous. Letting \(V\) be a directed subset of \(\mathsf{L} {A}\), we compute: \[\begin{aligned} d \bigsqcup V & = \bigsqcup \, {\left ( {\left \{ d_ \bot \right \}} \cup {\left \{ d_Aa \mid \bigsqcup V = \eta _A a \right \}} \right)} \\ & = \bigsqcup \, {\left ({\left \{ d_ \bot \right \}} \cup {\left \{ d_Aa \mid \eta _Aa \in V \right \}} \right)} \\ & = \bigsqcup {\left \{ \bigsqcup {\left ({\left \{ d_ \bot \right \}} \cup {\left \{ d_Aa \mid a ^ {?} = \eta _Aa \right \}} \right)} \mid a ^ {?} \in V \right \}} \\ & = \bigsqcup {\left \{ da ^ {?} \mid a ^ {?} \in V \right \}} \end{aligned} \]
For uniqueness, suppose that we have two morphisms \(\mathsf{L} {A} \xrightarrow{d,d'} D\) factoring our square in the sense described above and fix \(a ^ {?} : \mathsf{L} {A}\): \[\begin{aligned} d a ^ {?} & = d \bigsqcup \, {\left ({\left \{ \bot \right \}} \cup {\left \{ \eta _Aa \mid a ^ {?} = \eta _Aa \right \}} \right)} \\ & = \bigsqcup \, {\left ({\left \{ d \bot \right \}} \cup {\left \{ d \, {\left (\eta _Aa \right)} \mid a ^ {?} = \eta _Aa \right \}} \right)} \\ & = \bigsqcup \, {\left ({\left \{ d_ \bot \right \}} \cup {\left \{ d_Aa \mid a ^ {?} = \eta _Aa \right \}} \right)} \\ & = \bigsqcup \, {\left ({\left \{ d' \bot \right \}} \cup {\left \{ d' \, {\left (\eta _Aa \right)} \mid a ^ {?} = \eta _Aa \right \}} \right)} \\ & = d' \bigsqcup \, {\left ({\left \{ \bot \right \}} \cup {\left \{ \eta _Aa \mid a ^ {?} = \eta _Aa \right \}} \right)} \\ & = d'a ^ {?} \end{aligned} \]

mainmatter.p: Let \(A_ \bullet\) be a diagram of dcpos indexed in a connected category \(\mathcal{I}\). Then the lifting endofunctor \(\mathsf{L}\) on dcpos preserves the colimit of \(A_ \bullet\).

details.p: Let \(A_ \bullet \xrightarrow{\iota _ \bullet} {\left \{ A_ \infty \right \}}\) be a universal cocone; we must show that \(\mathsf{L} {A_ \bullet} \xrightarrow{\mathsf{L} {\iota _ \bullet}} {\left \{ \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty} \right \}}\) is universal as well, i.e. show any cocone \(\mathsf{L} {A_ \bullet} \xrightarrow{d_ \bullet} {\left \{ D \right \}}\) factors uniquely through \(\mathsf{L} {A_ \bullet} \xrightarrow{\mathsf{L} {\iota _ \bullet}} {\left \{ \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty} \right \}}\) in the following sense:

Using the universal property of \(\mathsf{L} {A_ \infty}\) as a co-comma dcpo, we may define \(\mathsf{L} {A_ \infty} \xrightarrow{d_ \infty} D\) to be the universal map determined by a certain lax square in the following configuration:

First we define \({\mathbf{1} _ {}} \xrightarrow{h_ \bot} {D}\) to be the element \(d_i \bot\) determined by an arbitrary object \(i \in \mathcal{I}\). To see that this is well-defined, fix \(i,j \in \mathcal{I}\) to check that \(d_i \bot =d_j \bot\). As \(\mathcal{I}\) is connected, we may proceed by induction on a zigzag \(i \leadsto j \in \mathcal{I}\); ultimately, this amounts to checking that for any span \(i \leftarrow k \rightarrow j\) in \(\mathcal{I}\), we have \(d_i \bot =d_k \bot =d_j \bot\). This follows from the strictness of lifted morphisms as well as the naturality of the cocone \(d_ \bullet\).
Next we define \({A_ \infty} \xrightarrow{h _ {A_ \infty}} {D}\) using the universal property of the colimiting cocone \({A_ \bullet} \xrightarrow{\iota _ \bullet} {A_ \infty}\):

To arrange \(h _ {A_ \infty}\) and \(h_ \bot\) into the desired lax square, we must check that for all \(a_ \infty :A_ \infty\) we have \(h_ \bot \sqsubseteq h _ {A_ \infty} a_ \infty\). Fixing arbitrary \(i \in \mathcal{I}\) and \(a \in A_i\), it suffices to check that \(h_ \bot \sqsubseteq h _ {A_ \infty} \iota _ {i} a\). As \(h_ \bot =d_i \bot\) and \(h _ {A_ \infty} \iota _ia = d_i \eta _ {A_i} a\), our goal follows directly from the monotonicity of \({\mathsf{L} {A_i}} \xrightarrow{d_i} {D}\).
Thus we have the desired lax square and a unique morphism \({\mathsf{L} {A_ \infty}} \xrightarrow{h} {D}\) factoring it through the co-comma square that defines \(\mathsf{L} {A_ \infty}\):

Our goal is to show that we may choose the universal map \({\mathsf{L} {A_ \infty}} \xrightarrow{d_ \infty} {D}\) corresponding to the cocone \({\mathsf{L} {A_ \bullet}} \xrightarrow{d_ \bullet} {{\left \{ D \right \}}}\) to be the morphism \(h\) defined above. First we must prove that the following triangle commutes:
Using the universal property of the co-comma squares for each \(\mathsf{L} {A_i}\), it suffices to check that two diagrams commute:

li.p: We must check that the outer triangle below commutes:
Rewriting using the strictness of lifted morphisms, this amounts to the established fact that \(h \bot = h_ \bot\).
We must check that the outer triangle below commutes:
Rewriting with established equations, it suffices to observe that the outer diagram below commutes:

ol.p: Finally, we must check that any two morphisms \({\mathsf{L} {A_ \infty}} \xrightarrow{k,k'} {D}\) factoring \({\mathsf{L} {A_ \bullet}} \xrightarrow{d_ \bullet} {{\left \{ D \right \}}}\) through \({\mathsf{L} {A_ \bullet}} \xrightarrow{\mathsf{L} {\iota _ \bullet}} {{\left \{ \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty} \right \}}}\) are equal. To that end, we will use the universal property of the co-comma square defining \(\mathsf{L} {A_ \infty}\) once more to reduce this to checking that \(k,k'\) agree on \(\bot\) and on \(\eta _ {A_ \bullet}\). The former follows directly from the strictness of \(\mathsf{L} {\iota _ \bullet}\), as we have \(k \bot = k {\left (\mathsf{L} \iota _ \bullet \bot \right)} =d_ \bullet \bot\). For the latter, we must check that \(k \circ \eta _ {A_ \infty} =k' \circ \eta _ {A_ \infty}\); by the universal property of the colimiting cocone \({A_ \bullet} \xrightarrow{\iota _ \bullet} {A_ \infty}\), it suffices to check that \(k \circ \eta _ {A_ \infty} \circ \iota _ \bullet = k' \circ \eta _ {A_ \infty} \circ \iota _ \bullet\). By naturality of the unit, these are both equal to \(d_ \bullet \circ \eta _ {A_ \bullet}\).

mainmatter.p: Let \(\mathbb{L} = {\left (\mathsf{L} , \eta , \mu \right)}\) be the lifting monad on dcpos; the Eilenberg-Moore category \(\mathsf{dcpo} ^ {\mathbb{L}}\) is closed under connected colimits, and these colimits are created by the forgetful functor \({\mathsf{dcpo} ^ {\mathbb{L}}} \xrightarrow{\mathsf{U} ^ {\mathbb{L}}} {\mathsf{dcpo}}\).

details.p: This follows from the following facts:

ol.li: The category of dcpos is (even constructively) known to be cocomplete.
Lifting preserves connected colimits.
The forgetful functor \({\mathsf{dcpo} ^ {\mathbb{L}}} \xrightarrow{\mathsf{U} ^ {\mathbb{L}}} {\mathsf{dcpo}}\) creates whatever colimits \(\mathsf{L}\) preserves.

mainmatter.p: These notes were prepared by Jonathan Sterling, based in part on discussions with Martín Escardó and Tom de Jong. Any errors belong to Jonathan Sterling.

frontmatter.title: The Positive Elements of a Pointed Dcpo

mainmatter.p: An element \(u\) of a pointed dpco \(A\) is called positive when for any semidirected subset \(I \subseteq A\), if \(u \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup{I}\) then \(I\) is inhabited. We will write \(\mathsf{pos} \,{u}\) to mean that \(u\) is a positive element.
(This is adapted from Johnstone’s definition of positive elements of frames to the case of dcpos by Tom de Jong.)

frontmatter.title: The Bottom Element is not Positive

mainmatter.p: The bottom element \(\bot\) of a pointed dcpo \(A\) is not positive.

details.p: Suppose that \(\bot\) was positive. The empty set \({\left \{ \right \}} \subseteq A\) is semidirected and \(\bot \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup {\left \{ \right \}} = \bot\), but nonetheless \({\left \{ \right \}}\) is not inhabited.

mainmatter.p: Let \(i \sqsubseteq j\) in a pointed dcpo \(A\) such that \(i\) is positive; then \(j\) is positive.

details.p: Fixing semidirected \(J \subseteq A\) such that \(j \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup{J}\), we must show that \(J\) is inhabited. This follows from our assumption that \(i\) is positive becuase \(i \sqsubseteq j \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup{J}\).

frontmatter.title: Closure of Positivity Under Directed Suprema

mainmatter.p: Let \(A\) be a pointed dcpo; the positivity predicate \({\Gamma A} \xrightarrow{\mathsf{pos}} {\Omega}\) is closed under directed suprema.

details.p: Let \(J \subseteq A\) be a directed subset such that every \(u \in J\) is positive; we must show that \(\bigsqcup{J}\) is positive. Fixing a subdirected subset \(I \subseteq A\) such that \(\bigsqcup{J} \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup I\), we must check that \(I\) is inhabited. The inequality \(\bigsqcup{J} \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup I\) holds if and only if \(u \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup{I}\) for all \(u \in u\); as \(J\) is directed, we therefore have some positive \(u \in A\) such that \(u \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup{I}\) from which it follows that \(I\) is inhabited.

frontmatter.title: The Subdcpo Spanned by Positive Elements

mainmatter.p: Let \(A\) be a pointed dcpo; then the subposet \(A^+ \subseteq A\) spanned by positive is a dcpo.

details.p: This follows from the closure of positive elements in an algebra under directed suprema.

frontmatter.title: The Positive Part of a Semidirected Subset is Semidirected

mainmatter.p: Let \(I \subseteq A\) be a semidirected subset of a pointed dcpo \(A\); then the intersection \(I \cap A^+\) is semidirected, where \(A^+\) is the subdcpo spanned by positive elements.

details.p: Let \(i,j\) be two positive elements of \(I\); we must show that there exists a positive element \(k \in I\) such that \(i \sqsubseteq k\) and \(j \sqsubseteq k\). This follows immediately from the directedness of \(I\) and the fact that positivity is monotone.

frontmatter.title: Positivity = Definedness in a Lifted Dcpo

mainmatter.p: The positive elements of a lifted dcpo \(\mathsf{L} {A}\) are exactly the defined elements, i.e. those lying in the image of \({A} \xrightarrow{\eta} {\mathsf{L} {A}}\).

details.p: Let \(a \in A\) be an element of the dcpo \(A\). To show that \(\eta _A a\) is positive, we fix a semidirected subset \(U \subseteq \mathsf{L} {A}\) such that \(\eta _A a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup U\) to show that \(U\) is inhabited. The inequality implies that \(\top \sqsubseteq \exists u \in U. u {\downarrow}\), so we know that \(U\) is inhabited.

Conversely, suppose that \(u \in \mathsf{L} {A}\) is a positive element. Let \(U\) be the semidirected family \({\left \{ v \mid v = u \land u \downarrow \right \}}\); we evidently have \(u \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup{U}\), so \(U\) is inhabited. Thus it follows that \(u\) is defined.

frontmatter.title: Characterization of Positivity in Terms of Partial Elements

mainmatter.p: Let \(A\) be a pointed dcpo. Then an element \(a:A\) is positive if and only if for any partial element \(u: LA\) such that \(a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _ {p:u {\downarrow}} u [p]\), we have \(u {\downarrow} = \top\).

details.p: Therefore, we first suppose that any \(u: LA\) is defined supposing \(a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _ {p:u {\downarrow}} u [p]\) to check that \(a\) is positive. Fixing a semidirected subset \(I \subseteq A\) such that \(a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup{I}\), we must show that \(I\) is inhabited. We consider the partial element \(u= {\left (\exists i \in I. \top , \lambda \_ . \bigsqcup{I} \right)} :LA\), and observe that \(\bigsqcup{I} = \bigsqcup _ {i \in I} \bigsqcup{I} = \bigsqcup _ {p:u {\downarrow}} u [p]\), and so we have \(a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _ {p:u {\downarrow}} u [p]\) and thus it follows that \(u {\downarrow} = \top\) and so \(I\) is inhabited.

Conversely, if \(a\) is positive, it follows that any partial element \(u\) is defined when \(a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _ {p:u {\downarrow}} u [p]\), because the subset \({\left \{ y \mid y=a \land u {\downarrow} \right \}}\) is semidirected.

mainmatter.p: It follows from our characterization of positivity in terms of partial elements that an element \(a: A\) of a pointed dcpo \(A\) is positive if and only if it is strictly above the bottom element, i.e. \(\bot \sqsubset a\).
(See also Proposition 6.2.12 of Tom de Jong’s thesis.)

So far we have studied positivity as an order-theoretic property of domains; in this section, we explore a non-order-theoretic generalization of this condition that is applicable in the setting of axiomatic domain theory. This notion is inspired by the alternative characterization positivity in terms of partial elements.

frontmatter.title: The Sierpiński Interval of a Dominion

mainmatter.p: Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a category with pullbacks, a terminal object and let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a dominion on \(\mathcal{C}\). A Sierpiński space for \(\mathcal{M}\) is defined to be a universal \(\mathcal{M}\)-partial map, i.e. a morphism \({\mathbf{1} _ {\mathcal{C}}} \xrightarrow{\top} {\Sigma} \in \mathcal{M}\) such that for any \({U} \xrightarrow{i} {A} \in \mathcal{M}\) there exists a unique map \({A} \xrightarrow{\chi _i} {\Sigma}\) making the following square cartesian:

When \(\mathcal{C}\) has an initial object \(\mathbf{0} _ {\mathcal{C}}\) and every \({\mathbf{0} _ {\mathcal{C}}} \xrightarrow{} {A}\) lies in \(\mathcal{M}\), the Sierpiński space becomes an “interval object” \({\left (\Sigma , \top , \bot \right)}\) where \({\mathbf{1} _ {\mathcal{C}}} \xrightarrow{\bot} {\Sigma}\) is given as follows:
Under these circumstances, we refer to \(\mathcal{M}\) as a dominion that has a Sierpiński interval.

Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be category with pullbacks, a terminal object, and an initial object, equipped with a dominion \(\mathcal{M}\) that has a Sierpiński interval \({\left (\Sigma , \top , \bot \right)}\). A path from \({I} \xrightarrow{f} {C}\) to \({I} \xrightarrow{g} {C}\) in \(\mathcal{C}\) is defined to be a morphism \({\Sigma \times I} \xrightarrow{p} {C}\) such that the following diagram commutes:
We shall write \(p:f \leadsto g\) to denote a path from \(f\) to \(g\).

Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be category with pullbacks, a terminal object, and an initial object, equipped with a dominion \(\mathcal{M}\) that has a Sierpiński interval \({\left (\Sigma , \top , \bot \right)}\), such that \(\mathcal{M}\)-partial map classifiers \(LA\) exist for all \(A \in \mathcal{C}\). We will write \(\mathbb{L} = {\left (L, \eta , \mu \right)}\) for the resulting partial map classifier monad on \(\mathcal{C}\).

Let \({\left (A, \alpha \right)}\) be an \(\mathbb{L}\)-algebra in \(\mathcal{C}\), and let \({C} \xrightarrow{x} {A}\) be a generalized element of \(A\). We say that \({C} \xrightarrow{x} {A}\) is positive when for any \({C} \xrightarrow{u} {LA}\) such that there exists a path from \({C} \xrightarrow{x} {A}\) to \({C} \xrightarrow{u; \alpha} {A}\), then \({C} \xrightarrow{u} {LA}\) factors through some (necessarily unique) \({C} \xrightarrow{y} {A}\) as below:

frontmatter.title: The two Notions of Positivity Coincide for Pointed Dcpos

mainmatter.p: Let \(A\) be a pointed dcpo. Then an element \(x:A\) is a positive element of the pointed dcpo if and only if \({\mathbf{1} _ {}} \xrightarrow{x} {A}\) is a positive element in the dominion \(\mathcal{O}\) of Scott-open immersions of dcpos.

details.p: We first observe that the latter condition is equivalent to the following:
details.blockquote: For any \(u \in LA\), if \(a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _ {p: u {\downarrow}} u [p]\) then \(u {\downarrow} = \top\).
details.p: This follows from the (constructive) characterization of the path order in dcpos. Therefore, our result follows from our earlier characterization of positive elements of a pointed dcpo in terms of partial elements.

frontmatter.title: Positive Generation and Open Positivity Predicates

mainmatter.p: In a lifted dcpo \(\mathsf{L} {A}\), the positive elements form an open subdcpo \({\left (\mathsf{L} {A} \right)} ^+ \cong A\): thus, it follows that positivity is always an open property in classical mathematics. On the other hand, it seems that constructive mathematics does not necessarily assert that the positive elements form an open subspace in general. In this section, we grapple with this subtlety by studying the relationship between openness of \(A^+\) and a desirable property that we shall refer to as positive generation, which states that every element of \(A\) is the supremum of its “positive part”.
We say that a pointed dcpo \(A\) has an open positivity predicate if and only if any of the following equivalent conditions hold:

ol.li: The positivity predicate \({\Gamma A} \xrightarrow{\mathsf{pos}} {\Omega}\) tracks a continuous morphism of dcpos \({A} \xrightarrow{\mathsf{pos}} {\Sigma}\).
The embedding \({A^+} \xrightarrow{\iota ^+} {A}\) is a Scott-open immersion.
For a directed subset \(I \subseteq A\), if \(\bigsqcup{A}\) is positive then there exists an element \(a \in I\) that is positive in \(A\).

mainmatter.p: Let \(A\) be a dcpo; then an element \(a:A\) is called positively generated when it is the supremum of the semidirected subset \({\left \{ b:A \mid b=a \land \mathsf{pos} \,{a} \right \}}\), i.e. we have \(a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _ {p: \mathsf{pos} \, a} a\).
An element \(a\) of a pointed dcpo \(A\) is called non-negatively generated when \(a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _ {\lnot {\left (a= \bot \right)}} a\) holds.

frontmatter.title: Positively Generated Elements are Non-negatively Generated

mainmatter.p: Let \(a\) be a positively generated element of a pointed dcpo \(A\). Then \(a\) is also non-negatively generated.

details.p: We must check that \(a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _ {p: \lnot {\left (a = \bot \right)}} a\). By assumption, it suffices to check that \(\bigsqcup _ {p: \mathsf{pos} \, a} a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _ {p: \lnot {\left (a= \bot \right)}} a\), which follows because the bottom element is not positive.

frontmatter.title: Bottom and Non-bottom Elements are Non-negatively Generated

mainmatter.p: In a pointed dcpo, any non-bottom element is non-negatively generated.

ol.li: The bottom element \(\bot\).
Any compact element \(a:A\) for which \(\lnot {\left (a= \bot \right)}\) holds.

mainmatter.p: Hence, any element \(a\) for which it is decidable whether \(a= \bot\) is non-negatively generated.

details.p: The bottom element is clearly non-negatively generated, as it lies beneath any element. Now fix \(a:A\) and assume \(\lnot {\left (a= \bot \right)}\); we must check that \(a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _ {p: \lnot {\left (a= \bot \right)}} a\) but the latter is equal to \(a\).

mainmatter.p: When a pointed dcpo \(A\) satisfies either of the following equivalent conditions, we will refer to it as positively generated.

ol.li: Every element \(a: A\) is positively generated, i.e. we have \(a = \bigsqcup _ {p: \mathsf{pos} \, a} a\).
For any semidirected subset \(I \subseteq A\), we have \(\bigsqcup I = \bigsqcup {\left \{ a \in I \mid \mathsf{pos} \, a \right \}}\).

section.details:
Proof.
(2) implies (1) by setting \(I = {\left \{ a \right \}}\). Conversely, assume that each element is the supremum of its positive part and fix a semidirected subset \(I \subseteq A\). We compute: \[\bigsqcup {\left \{ a \in I \mid \mathsf{pos} \, a \right \}} = \bigsqcup _ {a \in {\left \{ a \in I \mid \mathsf{pos} \, a \right \}}} a = \bigsqcup _ {a \in I} \bigsqcup _ {p \in \mathsf{pos} \, a} a = \bigsqcup _ {a \in I} a \]

frontmatter.title: Positively Generated Dcpos Have Open Positivity Predicates

mainmatter.p: Let \(A\) be a positively generated pointed dcpo. Then \(A\) has an open positivity predicate.

details.p: Let \(I \subseteq A\) be a directed subset such that \(\bigsqcup{I}\) is positive; we must find a positive element of \(I\). Because \(A\) is positively generated we know that \(\bigsqcup{I} \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup {\left (I \cap A^+ \right)}\). Because \(\bigsqcup{I}\) is positive and \(I \cap A^+\) is semidirected, it follows that the latter is inhabited.

mainmatter.p: Let \(A\) be a dcpo; then the lift \(\mathsf{L} {A}\) is positively generated.

details.p: Let \(u= {\left (\phi ,a \right)}\) be an element of \(\mathsf{L} {A}\). Note that \(u\) is equal to \(\bigsqcup _ {p: \phi} u\); as definedness coincides with positivity in lifted dcpos, we have \(\phi = \mathsf{pos} \, u\), and so we are done.

frontmatter.title: Lifted Dcpos Have Open Positivity Predicates

mainmatter.p: Let \(A\) be a dcpo; then the lifting \(\mathsf{L} {A}\) has an open positivity predicate.

details.p: It suffices to recall that a lifted dcpo is positively generated.

frontmatter.title: When is a Continuous Pointed Dcpo Positively Generated?

mainmatter.p: We describe some light conditions under which a continuous dcpo is positively generated.

frontmatter.title: Non-negatively Generated Basis Yields Positive Generation

mainmatter.p: Let \(A\) be a pointed continuous dcpo presented by a basis \(B \subseteq A\) such that each element of \(B\) is non-negatively generated in \(A\). Then \(A\) is positively generated.

details.p: We must show that for any \(a \in A\), we have \(a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _ {p: \mathsf{pos} \, a} a\). Let \(B_a\) be the subset \({\left \{ b \in B \mid b \ll a \right \}}\). By assumption, it suffices to check that \(\bigsqcup B_a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _ {p: \mathsf{pos} \, a} a\). Fixing \(b \in B_a\), we must check that \(b \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _ {p: \mathsf{pos} \, a} a\).

As \(b\) is assumed to be non-negatively generated, we have \(b= \bigsqcup _ {p: \lnot {\left (b= \bot \right)}} b\) so to check \(b \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _ {p: \mathsf{pos} \, a} a\) it suffices to check that \(\lnot {\left (b= \bot \right)}\) implies that \(a\) is positive. We fix semidirected \(J \subseteq A\) such that \(a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup{J} = \bigsqcup {\left ({\left \{ \bot \right \}} \cup J \right)}\) to check that \(J\) is inhabited. We recall that \(b \ll a\) and \(a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup {\left ({\left \{ \bot \right \}} \cup J \right)}\), so it follows by the definition of the way-below relation that either \(b \sqsubseteq \bot\) or there exists \(j \in J\) such that \(b \sqsubseteq j\). The first case is contradictory by our assumption that \(\lnot {\left (b= \bot \right)}\), and the second case verifies that \(J\) is inhabited, so \(a\) is positive.

frontmatter.title: Basis With Decidable Bottomhood Yields Positive Generation

mainmatter.p: Let \(A\) be a pointed continuous dcpo presented by a basis \(B \subseteq A\) for each element \(b \in B\) it is decidable whether \(b= \bot\). Then \(A\) is positively generated.

details.p: We observe that every element of \(B\) is non-negatively generated, so we may conclude that \(A\) is positively generated.

frontmatter.title: Positively Generated Basis Yields Positive Generation

mainmatter.p: Let \(A\) be a pointed continuous dcpo presented by a basis \(B \subseteq A\) whose elements are all positively generated in \(A\). Then \(A\) is positively generated.

details.p: As positive generation implies non-negative generation for an element of a pointed dcpo, we may conclude that \(A\) is positively generated.

mainmatter.p: I am not sure how useful the following construction is. On the other hand, maybe it has a universal property at the level of posets.

frontmatter.title: The Positively Generated Core of a Dcpo

mainmatter.p: Let \(A\) be a pointed dcpo; the subposet \(A ^ {\mathit{pg}} \subseteq{A}\)spanned by positively generated elements is closed under directed suprema, and thus gives rise to a subdcpo that we shall refer to as the positively generated core of \(A\).

details.p: Let \(I \subseteq A\) be a directed subset whose elements are all positively generated. We must check that the supremum \(\bigsqcup{I}\) is positively generated, i.e. \(\bigsqcup{I} \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _ {p: \mathsf{pos} \, \bigsqcup{I}} \bigsqcup{I}\). Fixing \(i \in I\), we must check that \(i \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _ {p: \mathsf{pos} \,{\bigsqcup{I}}} \bigsqcup{I}\). As \(i\) is positively generated, we have \(i \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _ {p: \mathsf{pos} \,{i}} i\). As positivity is upward-closed, we have \(\mathsf{pos} \,{i} \sqsubseteq \mathsf{pos} \, \bigsqcup{I}\). Therefore, we deduce \(i \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _ {p: \mathsf{pos} \, i} i \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _ {p: \mathsf{pos} \,{\bigsqcup{I}}} \bigsqcup{I}\).

frontmatter.title: Characterizing Freeness in Terms of Positivity

mainmatter.p: In classical mathematics, every \(\mathbb{L}\)-algebra is free; this does not appear to be asserted by constructive mathematics, but in this section we verify that freeness can be characterized in terms of positivity. The main result is that a pointed dcpo \(A\) is positively generated if and only the evident map \({\mathsf{L} {A^+}} \xrightarrow{} {A}\) is an isomorphism.

tree.mainmatter:
Let \(A\) be a pointed dcpo with an open positivity predicate; we define the “purification” map \({A} \xrightarrow{\mathsf{purify} _A} {\mathsf{L} {A^+}}\) into the lift of the subdcpo of positive elements to send \(a \in A\) to the partial element \({\left (\mathsf{pos} \,{a} , \lambda p. a \right)}\); we note:\[ \mathsf{purify} _Aa = {\left (\mathsf{pos} \,{a} , \lambda p. a \right)} = \bigsqcup _ {p: \mathsf{pos} \, a} \eta _Aa\]
The mapping defined above is continuous because we have assumed that \(A\) has an open positivity predicate, so its domain of definition defines a Scott-open subdcpo of \(A\).

frontmatter.title: Pointed Dcpos With Open Positivity Predicates are Almost Free

mainmatter.p: Let \(A\) be a pointed dcpo with an open positivity predicate; then the purification map \({A} \xrightarrow{\mathsf{purify} _A} {\mathsf{L} {A^+}}\) is the projection part of an embedding-projection pair \({\left (\mathsf{L} {\iota ^+} ; \alpha _A \right)} \dashv \mathsf{purify} _A\).

section.details:
Proof.
We fix \({\left (\phi ,a^+ \right)} : \mathsf{L} {A^+}\) and compute explicitly using the fact that \(\mathsf{purify} _A\) preserves the bottom element and therefore preserves semidirected subsets: \[\begin{aligned} \mathsf{purify} _A {\left (\alpha _A {\left (\mathsf{L} {\iota ^+} {\left (\phi ,a^+ \right)} \right)} \right)} & = \mathsf{purify} _A {\left (\alpha _A {\left (\phi ,a^+ \right)} \right)} \\ & = \mathsf{purify} _A \bigsqcup _ {p: \phi} a^+p \\ & = \bigsqcup _ {p: \phi} \mathsf{purify} _A {\left (a^+p \right)} \\ & = \bigsqcup _ {p: \phi} \bigsqcup _ {q: \mathsf{pos} {\left (a^+p \right)}} a^+p \\ & = \bigsqcup _ {p: \phi} a^+p \\ & = {\left (\phi ,a^+ \right)} \end{aligned} \]
For the deflation, we fix \(a:A\) and observe that \( \alpha _A {\left (\mathsf{L} {\iota ^+} {\left (\mathsf{purify} _A a \right)} \right)} = \bigsqcup _ {p: \mathsf{pos} \, a} a \) which is evidently below \(a\).

frontmatter.title: Positively Generated Pointed Dcpos are Free

mainmatter.p: Let \(A\) be a positively generated pointed dcpo; then \(A\) is canonically free on its subdcpo of positive elements \(A^+\). More specifically, the embedding-projection pair \({\left (\mathsf{L} {\iota ^+} ; \alpha _A \right)} \dashv {A} \xrightarrow{\mathsf{purify} _A} {\mathsf{L} {A^+}}\) given by the purification map is an isomorphism of dcpos.

details.p: First, we recall that the embedding-projection pair \({\left (\mathsf{L} {\iota ^+} ; \alpha _A \right)} \dashv \mathsf{purify} _A\) exists because \(A\) has an open positivity predicate by virtue of its positive generation. Therefore, the statement of this lemma is well-formed.

Because \({\left (\mathsf{L} {\iota ^+} ; \alpha _A \right)} \dashv \mathsf{purify} _A\) is an embedding-projection pair, it remains only to check that \(\mathsf{purify} _A; \mathsf{L} {\iota ^+} ; \alpha _A\) is an inflation, i.e. for all \(a:A\) we must have \(a \sqsubseteq \alpha _A {\left (\mathsf{L} {\iota ^+} {\left (\mathsf{purify} _Aa \right)} \right)}\). As the latter is equal to \(\bigsqcup _ {p: \mathsf{pos} \, a} a\), the inflation condition follows from our assumption that \(A\) is positively generated.

mainmatter.p: A pointed dcpo \(A\) is lifted if and only if it is positively generated.

details.p: This follows from two existing lemmas:

ol.li: Lifted dcpos (free algebras) are positively generated.
Positively generated pointed dcpos are free.

mainmatter.p: This document records what I have learned about the design of “tools for mathematical thought” over the past couple years. One of my goals in writing this is to set out both the unique requirements of an information data model that is needed to record and facilitate mathematical thought, as well as the technical requirements for tools that can be used for mathematics.

A “tool for mathematical thought” could be many things, but it must be a tool for the development and interlinking of mathematical ideas in a way that facilitates authoring, publishing, teaching, learning, and the maintenance of evergreen notes.

A tool for mathematical thought could be a piece of software, or it could be an organizing principle for physical notes on paper. In these notes, we will primarily explore the design of computerized tools for mathematical thought.

The existing tools for mathematical thought can be divided into two main categories: interactive proof assistants and textual authoring and publishing tools (including \(\LaTeX\), as well as the Gerby software that runs the Stacks Project).

The phrase evergreen note is due to Andy Matuschak, who has written extensively about it in his public Zettelkasten. Evergreen notes are permanent notes that evolve and accumulate over time, cutting across different projects.

One of the design principles for evergreen notes described by Matuschak is atomicity (Evergreen notes should be atomic): a note should capture just one thing, and if possible, all of that thing. A related point is that it should be possible to understand a note by (1) reading it, and (2) traversing the notes that it links to and recursively understanding those notes.

Traditional mathematical notes do not achieve this kind of atomicity: understanding the meaning of a particular node (e.g. a theorem or definition) usually requires understanding everything that came (textually) before it. In the context of the hierarchical organization of evergreen mathematical notes, this would translate to needing to go upward in the hierarchy in order to understand the meaning of a leaf node. I regard this property of traditional mathematical notes as a defect: we should prefer explicit context over implicit context.

High-quality mathematical notes should make sense without context; hierarchical context is imposed in order to tell a story, but consumers of mathematical notes should not be forced into a particular narrative. Indeed, as many different hierarchical structures can be imposed, many different narratives can be explored.

My first exploration of hypertext mathematics was the lecture notes on relative category theory; in hindsight, these lecture notes are very much traditional lecture notes, not written with the atomicity principle in mind. As a result, it is often difficult to understand a given node without ascending upward in the hierarchy.
Atomicity in evergreen mathematical notes is enhanced by adhering to the following principles:

ol.li: no free variables: do not rely on one-off objects that are defined incidentally upwards in the hierarchy; turn them into atomic nodes that can be linked;
favor explicit dependency: whenever using a terminology or construction that has been defined elsewhere, link it;
notation should be decodable: all notations (except the most very basic) should be recalled via a link.

mainmatter.p: It can be a bit excessive to link every word: but the pertinent links could be added to a “related pages” section.

Matuschak describes a number of organizing principles for evergreen notes, which are quite compelling; one design principle (Prefer associative ontologies to hierarchical taxonomies) deserves additional discussion in the context of mathematical thought. In particular, the problem of circular reference must be grappled with immediately rather than incidentally: in ordinary knowledge management, circularity represents the completion of a train of thought, whereas in mathematical thinking it remains very important to distinguish assumptions from consequences.

Hence a purely associative organization of mathematical knowledge is not viable (although it often happens by accident), and so the hierarchical organization of mathematics must be taken seriously from the start. We find that Matuschak has in fact already grappled with the need for hierarchy in his note It’s hard to navigate to unlinked “neighbors” in associative note systems, where he discusses the difficulty of traversing the “neighbor”-relationship between notes that are related by another note’s context, but are not related on their own. Matuschak proposes to solve the problem by grafting hierarchy onto the associative ontology after the fact through “outline notes”:

blockquote.p: “Outline notes” can create pseudo-hierarchies with order and structure by linking to many child notes. Then we need the UI to support navigating between neighbors “through” these outline notes.

mainmatter.p: The viewpoint of outline hierarchy as a structure imposed on the existing associative ontology is a convenient organizing principle for evergreen notes in the sense of Matuschak, but it is a necessary principle for the design of tools for mathematical thought.

Multiple hierarchical structures can be imposed on the same associative network of nodes; a hierarchical structure amounts to a “narrative” that contextualizes a given subgraph of the network. One example could be the construction of lecture notes; another example could be a homework sheet; a further example could be a book chapter or scientific article. Although these may draw from the same body of definitions, theorems, examples, and exercises, these objects are contextualized within a different narrative, often toward fundamentally different ends.

As a result, any interface for navigating the neighbor-relation in hierarchically organized notes would need to take account of the multiplicity of parent nodes. Most hypertext tools assume that the position of a node in the hierarchy is unique, and therefore have a single “next/previous” navigation interface; we must investigate the design of interfaces that surface all parent/neighbor relations.

frontmatter.title: The Best Structure to Impose is Relatively Flat

mainmatter.p: It is easy to make the mistake of prematurely imposing a complex hierarchical structure on a network of notes, which leads to excessive refactoring. Hierarchy should be used sparingly, and its strength is for the large-scale organization of ideas. The best structure to impose on a network of many small related ideas is a relatively flat one. I believe that this is one of the mistakes made in the writing of the foundations of relative category theory, whose hierarchical nesting was too complex and quite beholden to my experience with pre-hypertext media.
There are many ways to model hierarchy, but there are two salient orthogonal distinctions in the different designs.

frontmatter.title: Absolute vs. Relative Hierarchy in Document Markup Languages

mainmatter.p: Both HTML and \(\LaTeX\) support a form of hierarchical organization with “absolute” heading levels, i.e. levels that count upward from a single root. In HTML, there is <h1>, <h2>, <h3>..., and in \(\LaTeX\) there is \part, \chapter, \section, \subsection,\subsubsection, ..., \paragraph depending on the document class. This is in contrast to a relative model of hierarchy, in which there is a single command to introduce a section heading at the “current” level, and there are other commands to introduce hierarchical nesting.

The absolute sectioning model is completely inadequate for the hierarchical organization of ideas, for the simple reason that it is the context of a node that determines what its level in the hierarchy is, not the node itself. When this is mixed up, it makes re-contextualization an extremely painful and time-consuming process: you must recursively increment or decrement all section levels that occur underneath a given node, as anyone who has used \(\LaTeX\) for any significant writing project can attest.

In traditional texts, re-contextualization occurs when you want to move a section from one place in the hierarchy to another; in the more fluid media I am pursuing, there may be many orthogonal hierarchical structures imposed on the network, so re-contextualization ceases to be a refactoring task and is elevated as a basic unit of mathematical activity. In either case, we are drawn to prefer relative hierarchy over absolute hierarchy. See existing implementations of this idea.

This is similar to the relationship between De Bruijn levels (global levels) and De Bruijn indices (local levels) in type theory: conventional section headings are like De Bruijn indices in that they count from the root node, whereas what we would want are section headings that count from the present node.

frontmatter.title: Implicit vs. Explicit Hierarchy in Document Markup Languages

mainmatter.p: Many document markup languages, including \(\LaTeX\) and HTML, use sectioning commands that evince an implicit hierarchical structure: for instance, consider the following HTML code:

The above corresponds to the tree [Foo > [Bar > [Baz, Qux]], Boo]. On the other hand, it is also possible to consider a model in which the hierarchy is made explicit through the syntactical tree structure of the markup language. This style is also supported (but not forced) in HTML:
We greatly prefer the combination of (relative, explicit) hierarchy.
There are a few \(\LaTeX\) packages that implement relative hierarchy for sectioning as an alternative to the backward model of absolute hierarchy.

ul.li: The coseoul package implements relative sectioning commands; similar to the existing sectioning commands, an implicit hierarchy model is employed, leading to an imperative feel with commands like \levelup{<title>} and \leveldown{<title>}.
The modular package builds on coseoul to behave better under the transclusion of \(\LaTeX\) documents, introducing a \subimport command that is to be used instead of \input or \include.

On the other hand, the dieudonne package implements a form of relative sectioning with an explicit hierarchical model, i.e. one in which the syntactical nesting of \(\LaTeX\) environments induces the hierarchy.

mainmatter.p: There are some attempts to impose a (relative, explicit) hierarchical model in HTML by using <section> and only the <h1> heading command. In the HTML5 spec, this behavior was initially endorsed as part of the “outline” algorithm, but almost no vendors of browsers nor assistive technology have correctly implemented this behavior so it is dead on arrival.

frontmatter.title: Forest and Trees of Evergreen Notes

mainmatter.p: A forest of evergreen notes (or a forest for short) is loosely defined to be a collection of evergreen notes in which multiple hierarchical structures are allowed to emerge and evolve over time. Concretely, one note may contextualize several other notes via transclusion within its textual structure; in the context of a forest, we refer to an individual note as a tree.
Of course, a tree can be viewed as a forest that has a root node.

frontmatter.title: The Extent of a Tree in a Forest

mainmatter.p: The extent of a tree within a forest is the smallest set of trees closed under the following rules:

ol.li: \(T\) lies within the extent of \(T\).
If \(S\) is transcluded by \(T\), then any tree in the extent of \(S\) lies also in the extent of \(T\).

mainmatter.p: A forest of evergreen notes may in general contain the work of many authors: for example, the tree on constructive domain theory comprises mathematical contributions of not only the present author, but also Martín Escardó and Tom de Jong. However, the correct attribution of authorship to a given tree is more subtle than one might at first think.

To understand this subtlety, we first consider that each individual tree may contain both textual content and transcluded subtrees. Thinking inductively, a simple model of tree authorship would be to take the union of the authors of the immediate textual content and the authors of all trees within its extent. This model is incorrect, however, as authorship is usually taken to imply responsibility and endorsement, as can be seen by way of example from the ACM Policy on Authorship, Peer Review, Readership, and Conference Publication:

blockquote.p: They agree to be held accountable for any issues relating to the correctness or integrity of the work with the understanding that depending on the circumstances not all authors are necessarily held “equally” accountable. In the case of publications-related misconduct, it may be the case that penalties may vary for co-authors listed on a single publication.

mainmatter.p: In particular, although one person may be aware of and responsible for the content of a given tree, it would be unreasonable to require them to be responsible for any subsequent (and potentially erroneous!) re-contextualization of that tree in the forest. For this reason, authors must be distinguished from contributors in forests of evergreen notes.
An author of a tree within a forest is someone who satisfies the following conditions:

ol.li: They contributed intellectually to the immediate textual content of the tree, i.e. the non-transcluded content.
They can be held responsible for all the content within the tree, i.e. both the immediate textual content as well as the content of all the subtrees.

mainmatter.p: A contributor to a tree within a forest is someone who is an author of at least one tree lying within its extent.

Many non-\(\LaTeX\) hypertext tools boast some compatibility with mathematical typesetting: for instance, in any HTML-based tool it is possible to use MathML or, for better cross-browser support and easier authoring, import \(\KaTeX\) or MathJax. For instance: Logseq, Obsidian, and Notion all support rendering of \(\LaTeX\) math code using either \(\KaTeX\) or MathJax. Unfortunately, the “support” provided is so limited that it is not usable for a working mathematician — so it is somewhat puzzling why the support is present in the first place. Here we will discuss some fundamental requirements for any tool that aims to support mathematical note, without which it is not applicable for use by professionals.

Mathematical writing tends to involve a variety of notations which (1) can be difficult to typeset by hand, and (2) will likely change over time. The difficulty of hand-typesetting is somewhat less important than the propensity of notation to change over time: when we change notations within a given mathematical work, we must update every occurrence of the notation: but when the representation of the notation is unstructured, it is not in fact possible for a tool (e.g. find-and-replace) to detect every instance that needs to be updated. Therefore, it is mandatory that the representation of mathematical notations be structured.

\(\LaTeX\) allows authors to structure their notations very simply using macros, which can be introduced using \newcommand or \NewDocumentCommand. It is trivial to update all occurences of a notation by simply changing the definition of the corresponding macro.

Unfortunately, most tools that purport to support the inclusion of mathematical expressions do not have adequate support for macros. Both \(\KaTeX\) and MathJax have excellent support for configuring macros, but these configuration options are not available in most of the tools that build on \(\KaTeX\) and MathJax: for instance, Logseq and Obsidian and Notion all support embedding mathematics, but they do not support configuring macros. In fact there is a community plugin for Obsidian that adds this functionality, but it only supports imposing a global macro library on the entire “vault”, which is inadequate.

frontmatter.title: Notational Macros are Local, not Global

mainmatter.p: In \(\LaTeX\), macros are organized into packages that are then globally imported into a single document. Because a \(\LaTeX\) document comprises just one project and thus any transclusions (via \input or \include) are of components local to that one project, this model is adequate — although experienced users of \(\LaTeX\) are nonetheless all too aware of the difficulties of namespacing macro commands when interacting with multiple packages or document classes.
