Let \(E\) be displayed over \(B\), and let \(f:x \to y \in B\); a morphism \(\bar{f} : \bar{x} \xrightarrow [f] {} \bar{y}\) in \(E\) is called cocartesian over \(f\) when for any \(m:y \to u\) and \(\bar{h} : \bar{x} \xrightarrow [f;m] {} \bar{u}\) there exists a unique \(\bar{m} : \bar{y} \xrightarrow [m] {} \bar{u}\) with \(\bar{f} ; \bar{m} = \bar{h}\):
We use a “pushout corner” to indicate \(\bar{x} \to \bar{y}\) as a cocartesian morphism, a notation justified by our discussion of the dual self-indexing.

A displayed category \(E\) over \(B\) is a cocartesian fibration when for each \(f : x \to y \in B\) and \(\bar{x} \in E _ {x}\), there exists a displayed object \(\bar{y} \in E _ {y}\) and a cocartesian morphism \(\bar{f} : \bar{x} \xrightarrow [f] {} \bar{y}\).
Cocartesian fibrations are also known as opfibrations.

frontmatter.title: The Total Opposite of a Displayed Category

mainmatter.p: Let \(E\) be displayed over \(B\); we define its total opposite \(E ^ {\tilde{\mathsf{o}}}\) displayed over \(B ^ {\mathsf{o}}\) as follows:

ol.li: An object of \(E ^ {\tilde{\mathsf{o}}} _ {x}\) is given by an object of \(E _ {x}\).

Given \(f : x \to y \in B ^ {\mathsf{o}}\), a displayed morphism \(\bar{x} \xrightarrow [f] {} \bar{y}\) in \(E ^ {\tilde{\mathsf{o}}}\) is given by a displayed morphism \(\bar{y} \xrightarrow [f] {} \bar{x}\) in \(E\).

mainmatter.p: Warning. Do not confuse this construction with the opposite fibered category, which produces a displayed category over \(B\) and not \(B ^ {\mathsf{o}}\).

Let \(E\) be displayed over \(B\). Prove that the total category\(\widetilde{E ^ {\tilde{\mathsf{o}}}}\) is \({\left (\widetilde{E} \right)} ^ {\mathsf{o}}\), and its projection functor is \({\left (p _ {E} \right)} ^ {\mathsf{o}} : \widetilde{E} ^ {\mathsf{o}} \to B ^ {\mathsf{o}}\).

Let \(E\) be displayed over \(B\), and let \(f:x \to y \in B\). Prove that a morphism \(\bar{f} : \bar{x} \xrightarrow [f] {} \bar{y}\) is cartesian over \(f\) in \(E\) if and only if \(\bar{f} : \bar{y} \xrightarrow [f] {} \bar{x}\) is cocartesian over \(f\) in \(E ^ {\tilde{\mathsf{o}}}\).
Prove that a displayed category \(E\) is a cartesian fibration over \(B\) if and only if \(E ^ {\tilde{\mathsf{o}}}\) is a cocartesian fibration over \(B ^ {\mathsf{o}}\).

Recall that the fundamental self-indexing \(\underline{B}\) of a category \(B\) is a displayed category with \(\underline{B} _ {x} = {B} _ {/ x}\). Recall that \(\underline{B}\) is a cartesian fibration over \(B\) if and only if \(B\) has pullbacks. However, \(\underline{B}\) is unconditionally a cocartesian fibration.
Prove that the fundamental self-indexing \(\underline{B}\) is a cocartesian fibration for any category \(B\).
Dually to the fundamental self-indexing, every category \(B\) can also be displayed over itself via its coslices \(B ^ {\setminus x}\).
Let \(B\) be a category. Define the displayed category \(\overline{B}\) over \(B\) as follows:

ol.li: For \(x \in B\), define \(\overline{B} _ {x}\) as the collection of pairs \((\bar{x} \in B,p _ {x} :x \to \bar{x})\).
For \(f : x \to y \in B\), define \(\overline{B} _ {f}\) to be the collection of commuting squares in the following configuration:

mainmatter.p: Prove that \(\overline{B}\) is a cocartesian fibration if and only if \(B\) has pushouts.
Prove that the total category of \(\overline{B}\) is the arrow category \(B ^ {\to}\), and the projection is the domain functor.
Prove that \(\overline{B}\) is a cartesian fibration for any category \(B\).
A cartesian fibration \(E\) over \(B\) is said to be a right fibration when all displayed morphisms in \(E\) are cartesian.

Recall that for every \(b \in B\), the collection of displayed objects \(E _ {b}\) and vertical maps \(E _ {1 _ {b}}\) forms a category. When \(E\) is a right fibration over \(B\), this category is in fact a groupoid.
A cartesian fibration \(E\) over \(B\) is a right fibration if and only if all its vertical maps are isomorphisms.

details.p: Suppose that \(E\) is a right fibration over \(B\), and fix \(b \in B\), \(\bar{b} \in E _ {b}\), and a vertical map \(f: \bar{b} \xrightarrow [1 _ {b}] {} \bar{b}\). Using the hypothesis that \(f\) is cartesian, it has a unique section \(g: \bar{b} \xrightarrow [1 _ {b}] {} \bar{b}\) as follows:
Likewise, because \(g\) is cartesian, \(f\) is the unique section of \(g\); thus \(f\) is an isomorphism in \(E _ {b}\).

Conversely, suppose that \(E\) is a cartesian fibration whose vertical maps are isomorphisms. Fix \(f:x \to y \in B\) and an arbitrary displayed morphism \(\bar{g} : \bar{x} \xrightarrow [f] {} \bar{y}\). Then \(\bar{g}\) is the precomposition of a cartesian lift \(\bar{f} : \bar{x} ' \xrightarrow [f] {} \bar{y}\) with a vertical map:

Because vertical maps are isomorphisms and \(\bar{f}\) is cartesian, we can observe that \(\bar{g}\) is cartesian as follows, writing \(\bar{m} : \bar{u} \xrightarrow [m] {} \bar{x} '\) for the unique factorization of \(\bar{h}\) through \(\bar{f}\) over \(m\):

mainmatter.p: Working in univalent foundations, let \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V}\) be a pair of not-necessarily-univalent universes such that \(\mathcal{U}\) is closed under \(\mathcal{V}\)-small products of \(\mathcal{U}\)-small types; then the subuniverse inclusion \({\mathcal{U}} \xrightarrow{j} {\mathcal{V}}\) is reflective. This argument will combine ideas due to Awodey, Frey, Speight, and Shulman. For any type \(A: \mathcal{V}\), we will define the reflection of \(A\) in \(\mathcal{U}\) in several steps.

Let \(\mathcal{U}\) be a subuniverse of \(\mathcal{V}\) and let \(A: \mathcal{V}\) be a type. A reflection algebra \(X\) under \(A\) in \(\mathcal{U}\) is defined to be a type \(UX: \mathcal{U}\) together with a function \({A} \xrightarrow{\eta _ {X}} {UX}\). A homomorphism \(f\) of reflection algebras from \(X\) to \(Y\) is given by a function \({UX} \xrightarrow{Uf} {UY}\) together with a homotopy \(\eta _ {f} : {\eta _ {Y}} \sim _ {A \to Y} {Uf \circ \eta _X}\).
When it causes no ambiguity, we will write \(X: \mathcal{U}\) for the underlying type \(UX\) of a given reflection algebra \(X\); likewise, we will write \(f x\) rather than \(Uf x\).

frontmatter.title: The Type of Reflection Algebras and Homomorphisms

mainmatter.p: We will write \({A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} : \equiv {\textstyle \sum _ {X: \mathcal{U}}} {A \to X}\) for the type of reflection algebras under \(A\); given two reflection algebras \(X,Y: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) we will write \({\left (X \multimap Y \right)} : \equiv {\textstyle \sum _ {f:X \to Y}} {\eta _ {Y}} \sim _ {A \to Y} {f \circ \eta _ {X}}\) for the type of homomorphisms of reflection algebras.

frontmatter.title: Identity and Composition of Reflection Algebra Homomorphisms

tree.mainmatter:
Let \(X: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) be a reflection algebra; we define the identity homomorphism \(1 _ {X} :X \multimap X\) on \(X\) as follows:\[ \begin{array} {l} 1 _ {X} x : \equiv x \\ \eta _ {1 _ {X}} a : \equiv \mathsf{refl} \end{array}\]
Now let \(Y,Z: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) be two further reflection algebras and let \(f : X \multimap Y\) and \(g : Y \multimap Z\) be two homomorphisms of reflection algebras. The composite homomorphism \(f;g : X \multimap Z\) is defined like so:\[ \begin{array} {l} {\left (f;g \right)} x : \equiv g {\left (fx \right)} \\ \eta _ {f;g} a : \equiv \mathsf{ap} _ {g} \, {\left (\eta _ {f} a \right)} \bullet \eta _ {g} a \end{array}\]

Given a pair of reflection algebras \(X,Y: {\mathcal{U}} \downarrow{A}\) and a pair of homomorphisms \(f,g : X \multimap  Y\), a homotopy from \(f\) to \(g\) is defined to be a homotopy \(\phi  :  {Uf}   \sim _ {X \to  Y}   {Ug}\) between the underlying functions together with a witness \(\bar{\phi}\)  of the following coherence:\[    \bar \phi  :
 {\textstyle \prod _ {a:A}} {   \eta _ {g} a
}  =_ {   {g {\left (\eta _ {X} a \right)}}  =_ {Y}   {\eta _ {Y} a}}   {   \eta _ {f} a \bullet   \phi {\left (\eta _ {X} a \right)}}\]
We shall write \({f}   \sim _ {X \multimap  Y}   {g}\) for the type of homotopies from the homomorphism \(f\) to the homomorphism \(g\).

mainmatter.p: Given a path of reflection algebras \(X,Y: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) and a homomorphism \(f : X \multimap Y\), the type \({\textstyle \sum _ {g:X \multimap Y}} {{g} \sim _ {X \multimap Y} {f}}\) of homomorphisms equipped with a homotopy to \(f\) is contractible, by function extensionality and the characterization of path spaces in dependent sums. Hence it follows that the canonical map \({f} =_ {X \multimap Y} {g} \to {f} \sim _ {X \multimap Y} {g}\) is an equivalence.

tree.mainmatter: Given a type \(K\) and a family of reflection algebras \(X : K \to {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) such that the dependent product \({\textstyle \prod _ {k:K}} U {\left (Xk \right)}\) lies in \(\mathcal{U}\), we may define the product of product of reflection algebras \({\textstyle \prod _ {k:K}} Xk\) to have the following structure map:\[ \begin{array} {l} \eta _ {{\textstyle \prod _ {k:K}} Xk} : A \to {\textstyle \prod _ {k:K}} {Xk} \\ \eta _ {{\textstyle \prod _ {k:K}} Xk} a : \equiv \lambda k:K. \, \eta _ {Xk} a \end{array}\]

mainmatter.p: Let \(X: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) be a reflection algebra; a displayed reflection algebra \(Y\) over \(X\) is defined to be a dependent type \(\bar{U} Y : X \to \mathcal{U}\) together with a dependent function \(\bar{\eta} _ {Y} : {\textstyle \prod _ {a:A}} {\bar{U} Y {\left (\eta _ {X} a \right)}} .\)
We will write \(Y [x]\) for \(\bar{U} Yx\).

tree.mainmatter:
Given a displayed reflection algebra \(Y\) over a reflection algebra \(X: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\), we may define the total reflection algebra \(\widetilde{Y} : {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) of \(Y\) as follows:\[ \begin{array} {l} U \widetilde{Y} : \equiv {\textstyle \sum _ {x:Ux}} Y [x] \\ \eta _ {\widetilde{Y}} a : \equiv {\left (\eta _ {X} a, \bar{\eta} _ {Y} a \right)} \end{array}\]
The projection function \({U \widetilde{Y}} \xrightarrow{\pi _1} {UX}\) extends to a homomorphism of reflection algebras \(\pi _ {Y} : \widetilde{Y} \multimap X\) as follows:\[ \begin{array} {l} \pi _ {Y} {\left (x,y \right)} : \equiv x \\ \alpha _ {\pi _ {Y}} a : \equiv \mathsf{refl} \end{array}\]

frontmatter.title: Displayed Reflection Algebras Over Displayed Reflection Algebras

mainmatter.p: If \(Y\) is a displayed reflection algebra over a reflection algebra \(X: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\), we define a displayed reflection algebra over \(Y\) to be a displayed reflection algebra in the original sense over the total reflection algebra \(\widetilde{Y}\) corresponding to \(Y\).

Letting \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V}\) be an impredicative pair of universes with \(A: \mathcal{V}\) a type in the outer universe, our goal is to construct a limit cone over the identity functor in the (∞,1)-category \({A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) of reflection algebras in \(\mathcal{U}\) under \(A: \mathcal{V}\), in order to prove that there is an initial reflection algebra. Even though we do not have access to the fully coherent language of (∞,1)-category theory in univalent foundations, we can specify the coherence structure that we need in order to obtain our result.

Our first step is describe a classifying reflection algebra for fully incoherent cones over the identity functor on \({A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\), which we do by means of a displayed reflection algebra of incoherent naturality structures on the product of all reflection algebras.

tree.mainmatter:
Let \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V}\) be an impredicative universe pair with \(A: \mathcal{V}\) a type in the outer universe, so that we may form the product \({\textstyle \prod _ {X: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}}} X\) of all reflection algebras in \(\mathcal{U}\) under \(A\). To express the data of the components of a given \(u: {\textstyle \prod _ {X: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}}} X\) being preserved by all homomorphisms and thus representing “cones” over the identity functor on \({A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\), we define a displayed reflection algebra \(\mathsf{INatStr}\) of “incoherent naturality structures” over \({\textstyle \prod _ {X: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}}} {X}\) as follows:\[ \begin{array} {l} \mathsf{INatStr} [u] : \equiv {\textstyle \prod _ {{\left \{ X,Y: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} \right \}}}} {\textstyle \prod _ {f : X \multimap Y}} {f {\left (uX \right)}} =_ {Y} {uY} \\ \bar{\eta} _ {\mathsf{INatStr}} \, a \, f : \equiv \eta _ {f} a \end{array}\]
Given an element \(u: \widetilde{\mathsf{INatStr}}\), we will write \(\epsilon _ {u} : {\textstyle \prod _ {X: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}}} X\) for the first component \(\pi _ {\mathsf{INatStr}} u\) and \(\vartheta _ {u} : \mathsf{INatStr} [\epsilon _ {u}]\) for the second component.
This definition is equivalent to one given by Awodey, Frey, and Speight.

mainmatter.p: As suggested by the name, the data of an incoherent naturality structure over a family \(u: {\textstyle \prod _ {X: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}}} UX\) is not particularly coherent. It will happen that for our goals, we need only add a single dimension of additional coherence data governing the behavior of the naturality structure on binary compositions of homomorphisms. This will give us a classifier for what we shall refer to as “semicoherent” cones over the identity functor on \({A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\).

tree.mainmatter:
Let \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V}\) be an impredicative universe pair with \(A: \mathcal{V}\) such that \(\mathsf{INatStr}\) is the displayed reflection algebra of incoherent naturality structures over the product \({\textstyle \prod _ {X: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}}} X\) of all reflection algebras. We define a further displayed reflection algebra \(\mathsf{SemiCoh}\) over \(\mathsf{INatStr}\) of “semicoherence structures” associating to each incoherent naturality structure one dimension of coherence data for binary compositions of homomorphisms.
In particular, we define \(\mathsf{SemiCoh}\) over \(\widetilde{\mathsf{INatStr}}\) as follows, employing our notion of homotopy between homomorphisms:\[ \begin{array} {l} \mathsf{SemiCoh} [u] : \equiv \\ \begin{array} {l} {\textstyle \prod _ {{\left \{ X,Y,Z: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} \right \}}}} {\textstyle \prod _ {f : X \multimap Y}} {\textstyle \prod _ {g : Y \multimap Z}} {\textstyle \prod _ {h : X \multimap Z}} {\textstyle \prod _ {\phi : {f;g} \sim _ {X \multimap Z} {h}}} \\ \mathsf{ap} _ {g} {\left (\vartheta _ {u} f \right)} \bullet \vartheta _ {u} g = \phi {\left (\epsilon _ {u} X \right)} \bullet \vartheta _ {u} h \end{array} \end{array}\]
To define the displayed algebra structure \(\bar \eta _ {\mathsf{SemiCoh}}\), we will use the contractibility of singletons several times. In particular, given \({\left (Y,f \right)}\) where \(Y: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) and \(f:X \multimap Y\), the algebra component \(\eta _ {Y}\) is completely determined by the homotopy \(\eta _ {f} : {\eta _ {Y}} \sim _ {A \to Y} {f \circ \eta _ {X}}\); likewise, by our characterization of paths between homomorphisms, the pair \({\left (h, \phi \right)}\) is uniquely determined. Therefore, we may define \(\bar \eta _ {\mathsf{SemiCoh}}\) to be the unique function satisfying the following equation:\[\bar \eta _ {\mathsf{SemiCoh}} \, a \, {\left (f, \lambda \_ . \mathsf{refl} \right)} \, {\left (g, \lambda \_ . \mathsf{refl} \right)} \, {\left ({\left (f;g \right)} , \lambda \_ . \mathsf{refl} \right)} , {\left (\lambda \_ . \mathsf{refl} , \lambda \_ . \mathsf{refl} \right)} := \mathsf{refl}\]
Given an element \(u: \widetilde{\mathsf{SemiCoh}}\), we shall write \(\epsilon _ {u} , \vartheta _ {u}\) for the components of its underlying incoherent naturality structure \(\pi _ {\mathsf{SemiCoh}} u\) and \(\varsigma _ {u} : \mathsf{SemiCoh} [\pi _ {\mathsf{SemiCoh}} u]\) for the semicoherence structure itself.
This definition is similar to that of Awodey, Frey, and Speight, except that we do not impose any coherence law for identities.

By the contractibility of singletons and our characterization of paths between homomorphisms, the definition of semicoherence structures is equivalent to following more direct formulation that avoids the additional cell \(\phi\):\[ {\textstyle \prod _ {{\left \{ X,Y,Z: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} \right \}}}} {\textstyle \prod _ {f : X \multimap Y}} {\textstyle \prod _ {g : Y \multimap Z}} \mathsf{ap} _ {g} {\left (\vartheta _ {u} f \right)} \bullet \vartheta _ {u} g = \vartheta _ {u} {\left (f;g \right)}\]
Our reason for choosing the more complex definition involving \({\left (h, \phi \right)}\) is that the extra flexibility comes in handy in our results, as suggested by Michael Shulman in private communication.

frontmatter.title: A Generic Cone Over the Identity Functor and its Generic Element

tree.mainmatter:
Letting \(I : \equiv \widetilde{\mathsf{SemiCoh}}\) be the total algebra of the displayed reflection algebra of semicoherence structures, we may define a dependent function\[ p _ {\bullet} : {\textstyle \prod _ {X: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}}} I \multimap X \]
exhibiting a “incoherent cone” from \(I\) onto the identity functor on \({A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\). In particular, we define the components of \(p _ {\bullet}\) as follows:\[ \begin{array} {l} p _ {X} \, u : \equiv \epsilon _ {u} X \\ \eta _ {p _ {X}} a : \equiv \mathsf{refl} \end{array}\]
Above we have used the definition of the structure map on the total reflection algebra as well as that of the displayed structure map on \(\mathsf{SemiCoh}\) over \(\mathsf{INatStr}\) over \({\textstyle \prod _ {X: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}}} X\). We shall refer to \(p _ {\bullet}\) as the generic cone over the identity functor, with the caveat that we have not formally established it as a “cone” of any kind. Furthermore, we shall refer to the component \(p _ {I} :I \multimap I\) as the generic element of the generic cone.

frontmatter.title: Quasi-idempotence of the Generic Element of the Generic Cone

tree.mainmatter:
The generic element \(p _ {I} :I \multimap I\) of the generic cone \(p _ {\bullet} : {\textstyle \prod _ {X: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}}} I \multimap X\) under \(I \equiv \widetilde{\mathsf{SemiCoh}}\) can be equipped with the structure of a pre-idempotent. In particular, we may define a homotopy \(\chi : {p _ {I} ;p _ {I}} \sim _ {I \multimap I} {p _ {I}}\) using the incoherent naturality structure of any element of \(I\).\[ \begin{array} {l} \chi \, u : \equiv \vartheta _ {u} p _ {I} \\ \bar{\chi} a : \equiv \mathsf{refl} \end{array}\]
That the higher identification \(\bar \chi{a} : \eta _ {p _ {I}} a = \eta _ {p _ {I} ;p _ {I}} \bullet \vartheta _ {\eta _ {I} a} p _ {I}\) may be defined by reflexivity follows from the definition of \(\eta _ {I} a\), the computation of composite homomorphisms, and the fact that \(\eta _ {p _ {I}} a\) is also defined by reflexivity; in particular, we compute:\[ \begin{aligned} \eta _ {p _ {I} ;p _ {I}} \bullet \vartheta _ {\eta _ {I} a} p _ {I} & \equiv {\left (\mathsf{ap} _ {p _ {I}} {\left (\eta _ {p _ {I}} a \right)} \bullet \eta _ {p _ {I}} a \right)} \bullet \eta _ {p _ {I}} a \\ & \equiv {\left (\mathsf{refl} \bullet \mathsf{refl} \right)} \bullet \mathsf{refl} \\ & \equiv \mathsf{refl} \bullet \mathsf{refl} \\ & \equiv \mathsf{refl} \end{aligned}\]
We will use the semicoherence structure associated to any element of \(I\) to exhibit the underlying function \(p _ {I} :I \to I\) of the generic element as a quasi-idempotent in the sense of Shulman. In particular, we must exhibit for each \(u:I\) an identification \(\psi : \mathsf{ap} _ {p _ {I}} {\left (\chi u \right)} = \chi {\left (p _ {I} u \right)}\). Unfolding definitions, this means to identify \(\mathsf{ap} _ {p _ {I}} {\left (\vartheta _ {u} p _ {I} \right)}\) with \(\vartheta _ {p _ {I} u} p _ {I}\).
From the semicoherence structure \(\varsigma _ {u} : \mathsf{SemiCoh} [\pi _ {\mathsf{SemiCoh}} u]\) we obtain the following identification:\[ \varsigma _ {u} \, p _ {I} \, p _ {I} \, p _ {I} \, \chi : \mathsf{ap} _ {p _ {I}} {\left (\vartheta _ {u} p _ {I} \right)} \bullet \vartheta _ {u} p _ {I} = \chi {\left (\epsilon _ {u} I \right)} \bullet \vartheta _ {u} p _ {I}\]
Canceling \(\vartheta _ {u} p _ {I}\), unfolding the definition of \(\chi\), and recalling that \(p _ {I} u \equiv \epsilon _ {u} I\) by definition, we obtain the desired identification \(\mathsf{ap} _ {p _ {I}} {\left (\vartheta _ {u} p _ {I} \right)} = \vartheta _ {p _ {I} u} p _ {I}\).
Therefore, the underlying function \(p _ {I} :I \to I\) has the structure of a quasi-idempotent.

frontmatter.title: Splitting the Generic Element of the Generic Cone

tree.mainmatter:
The underlying function \(p _ {I} :I \to I\) of the generic element \(p _ {I} :I \multimap I\) of the generic cone \(p _ {\bullet} : {\textstyle \prod _ {X: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}}} I \multimap X\) under \(I \equiv \widetilde{\mathsf{SemiCoh}}\) has been equipped with the structure of a quasi-idempotent in the sense of Shulman, so we may split it.
In particular, the construction of Shulman gives us a type \(J\) together with a retraction \(r : I \to J\) and a section \(s : J \to I\) witnessed by a homotopy \(\rho : {s;r} \sim _ {J \to J} {1 _ {J}}\), such that \(r;s \equiv p _ {I} : I \to I\) definitionally. Our goal is to lift this splitting of the underlying function \(p _ {I} :I \to I\) to a splitting of the homomorphism \(p _ {I} :I \multimap I\).
We may equip \(J\) with the structure of a reflection algebra by setting \(\eta _ {J} : \equiv \eta _ {I} ;r\), making \(r : I \multimap J\) a homomorphism of algebras definitionally, i.e. \(\eta _ {r} a: \equiv \mathsf{refl}\). To exhibit \(s : J \multimap I\) as a homomorphism of algebras, we may likewise use reflexivity:\[ \begin{array} {l} \eta _ {s} : {\eta _ {J} ;s} \sim _ {A \to I} {\eta _ {I}} \\ \eta _ {s} a : \equiv \mathsf{refl} \end{array}\]
That the above is well-defined can be deduced as follows, using the fact that the splitting of \(p _ {I} \equiv r;s\) is definitional and that \(p _ {I}\) is a definitional homomorphism:\[ \begin{aligned} s {\left (\eta _ {J} a \right)} & \equiv s {\left (r {\left (\eta _ {I} a \right)} \right)} \\ & \equiv p _ {I} {\left (\eta _ {I} a \right)} \\ & \equiv \eta _ {I} a \end{aligned}\]
We have to check that the homotopy of functions \(\rho : {s;r} \sim _ {J \to J} {1 _ {J}}\) lifts to a homotopy of homomorphisms. In particular, we must construct the coherence \(\bar \rho\) specified below:\[ \bar \rho : {\textstyle \prod _ {a : A}} \eta _ {1 _ {J}} a = \eta _ {s;r} a \bullet \rho {\left (\eta _ {J} a \right)}\]
Note that \(\eta _ {1 _ {J}}\) is reflexivity; we compute the other side of the desired identificiation \(\bar \rho a\) by unfolding definitions (e.g. that of identities and compositions of homomorphisms) and using basic path algebra:\[ \begin{aligned} \eta _ {s;r} a \bullet \rho {\left (\eta _ {J} a \right)} & \equiv {\left (\mathsf{ap} _ {r} {\left (\eta _ {s} a \right)} \bullet \eta _ {r} a \right)} \bullet \rho {\left (r {\left (\eta _ {I} a \right)} \right)} \\ & \equiv {\left (\mathsf{ap} _ {r} \mathsf{refl} \bullet \mathsf{refl} \right)} \bullet \rho {\left (r {\left (\eta _ {I} a \right)} \right)} \\ & \equiv {\left (\mathsf{refl} \bullet \mathsf{refl} \right)} \bullet \rho {\left (r {\left (\eta _ {I} a \right)} \right)} \\ & \equiv \mathsf{refl} \bullet \rho {\left (r {\left (\eta _ {I} a \right)} \right)} \\ & = \rho {\left (r {\left (\eta _ {I} a \right)} \right)} \end{aligned}\]
Therefore, to construct \(\bar \rho a\) is suffices to identify \(\rho {\left (r {\left (\eta _ {I} a \right)} \right)}\) with reflexivity. To do so, we will use an argument suggested by Shulman in private conversation. We recall from Theorem 3.3 of Shulman that the homotopy \(\rho\) induces a new witness of pre-idempotency for \(p _ {I}\), namely \(\chi ' x : \equiv \mathsf{ap} _ {s} {\left (\rho {\left (rx \right)} \right)}\) and that by Theorem 7.1 of the same, this homotopy may be identified with our original witness \(\chi : {p _ {I} ;p _ {I}} \sim _ {I \to I} {p _ {I}}\). Therefore we have \(\mathsf{ap} _ {s} {\left (\rho {\left (rx \right)} \right)} = \vartheta _ {x} p _ {I}\); because \(s\) is a section of \(r\), this implies that \(\rho {\left (r x \right)} = \mathsf{ap} _ {r} {\left (\vartheta _ {x} p _ {I} \right)}\) and so we may compute our identification \(\bar \rho \, a\) as follows:\[ \begin{aligned} \rho {\left (r {\left (\eta _ {I} a \right)} \right)} & = \mathsf{ap} _ {r} {\left (\vartheta _ {\eta _ {I} a} p _ {I} \right)} \\ & \equiv \mathsf{ap} _ {r} {\left (\eta _ {p _ {I}} a \right)} \\ & \equiv \mathsf{ap} _ {r} \mathsf{refl} \\ & \equiv \mathsf{refl} \end{aligned}\]
Above we have used the fact that the coherence datum \(\eta _ {p _ {I}} a\) is reflexivity.

mainmatter.p: Our main theorem then follows from wild categorical considerations, as we show below.

frontmatter.title: The Main Result: Reflectivity of Impredicative Subuniverses

mainmatter.p: Let \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V}\) be an impredicative pair of universes with \(A: \mathcal{V}\). There we may construct a reflection algebra \(A _ {\mathcal{U}} : {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) such that for any other reflection algebra \(X: {A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\), the space of homomorphisms \(A _ {\mathcal{U}} \multimap X\) is contractible.

details.p: Our splitting of the generic element of the generic cone \({\Delta I} \xrightarrow{p} {1 _ {{A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}}}\) over the identity functor on reflection algebras yields a new (incoherent) cone \({\Delta J} \xrightarrow{q} {1 _ {{A} \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}}}\) whose generic element \(q _ {J} :J \multimap J\) may be identified with the identity homomorphism. It follows that each space of homomorphisms \(J \to X\) is contractible. Therefore, we define \(A _ {\mathcal{U}}\) to be the apex \(J\).

mainmatter.p: If \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V}\) is an impredicative pair of universes, then \(\mathcal{U}\) is reflective in \(\mathcal{V}\).

details.p: Our construction of the initial reflection algebra for each \(A: \mathcal{V}\) is one of several equivalent formulations of reflective subuniverse in univalent foundations.

frontmatter.title: Dissertation Overview: First Steps in Synthetic Tait Computability

mainmatter.p: This is a chapter-by-chapter overview of the dissertation of Jonathan Sterling.

frontmatter.title: Background on Homotopy and Cubical Type Theory

mainmatter.p: For more than four decades, dependent type theory has been positioned as the “common language” that can finally unify mathematics and computer programming: while it has never been controversial that a computer program is a form of mathematical construction, the running hypothesis of the type theoretic community has been the converse to this claim, namely that mathematical constructions should be viewed as programs that can in principle be executed by a physical machine — roughly, sets = types and elements = programs. Thus the struggle to realize this type theoretic hypothesis has been a two-way process, punctuated by moments at which the mathematical meaning of a programming construct is elucidated, or at which the computational content of a mathematical construct is uncovered.

In the current millennium, a new identification has been taking shape in which types = \(\infty\)-groupoids (homotopy types), which are an infinite-dimensional generalization of sets; the origins of this new perspective on type theory lie with Hofmann and Streicher’s 1998 groupoid interpretation of type theory, combined with the revolutionary contributions of Voevodsky and Awodey and Warren respectively. The main feature of the new language, dubbed homotopy type theory or HoTT, is that isomorphisms between types are equipped with a new induction rule called univalence stating that all type theoretic constructs respect isomorphisms: to a first approximation, if \(A \cong B\) then \(P (A) \cong P (B)\) for any \(P\). The univalence principle is motivated by the phenomenon of homotopy invariance that pervades the large-scale structure of modern-day mathematics, from algebraic topology to algebraic geometry to mathematical physics; as a programming construct, univalence suggests new approaches to both generic and modular programming.

Thus one of the main projects for the first decade of homotopy type theory was to substantiate the relationship between HoTT and mathematics on the one hand, and between HoTT and computer programming on the other hand. The question of whether homotopy type theoretic language can be interpreted in sheaves on arbitrary infinite-dimensional spaces (\(\infty\)-topoi) has finally been resolved satisfactorily by Shulman in 2019. On the other hand, the computational interpretation of homotopy type theory has involved a reformulation of HoTT called cubical type theory that reorganizes the higher-dimensional structure discussed by considering all the points, lines, squares, cubes, hypercubes, and so-on that one can draw in a given type. The computational interpretation of the new cubical type theory can be split into two different conjectures:

For any closed term \(\cdot \vdash N: \mathsf{nat}\) of cubical type theory, there exists a unique natural number \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) such that \(\cdot \vdash N \equiv \bar{n} : \mathsf{nat}\) where \(\bar{n}\) is the encoding of the number \(n\) as a term in the type theory.
The assertions \(\Gamma \vdash {A} \ \textit{type}\), \(\Gamma \vdash {A \equiv B} \ \textit{type}\), \(\Gamma \vdash M:A\), and \(\Gamma \vdash M \equiv N: A\) are all decidable.

The canonicity conjecture ensures that terms written in cubical type theory can be thought of as computer programs, and was verified independently by Huber and Angiuli for different variants of cubical type theory. The decidability conjecture is no less important, as it is a necessary ingredient to implement a typechecker or a compiler for a programming language based on cubical type theory.

frontmatter.title: Contributions of First Steps in Synthetic Tait Computability

mainmatter.p: This dissertation positively resolves the decidability conjecture for cubical type theory, the last remaining open question in its syntactic metatheory. Standard techniques proved inadequate for tackling this problem, so the bulk of this dissertation focuses on developing a new mathematical technique called synthetic Tait computability that generalizes and abstracts the method of Tait computability or logical predicates; in the past two years, synthetic Tait computability has played a central role in solving several problems in both type theory and core programming languages, suggesting that this dissertation presents a lasting and transformative contribution to the state of the art.

frontmatter.title: Detailed Overview of First Steps in Synthetic Tait Computability

mainmatter.p: We give a chapter-by-chapter overview of First Steps in Synthetic Tait Computability. The novel contributions are contained in Chapters 4, 5, 7, and 8; the remaining Chapters 0-3 and 6 are primarily expository.

This chapter situates the motivations and applications of type theory in mathematics and computer science, and poses these against the semantic and syntactic properties of type theory that are needed to substantiate these applications. On the semantic side, type theory needs a number of properties including function extensionality, function comprehension, propositional univalence, effective quotients, etc.; on the syntactic side, type theory needs to be at least consistent, and many applications require both canonicity and decidability. Combining these syntactic and semantic properties into a single system has been a challenge, and cubical type theory was designed with the intention of satisfying them all. Prior to this dissertation, only the decidability conjecture remained open; thus with the present contribution, we regard the Cubical Hypothesis confirmed.

frontmatter.title: Chapter 1: Objective Syntax of Dependent Types

mainmatter.p: To state and prove theorems like canonicity and decidability for a type theory, we must have a mathematical definition of the syntax of type theory. Conventionally, the syntax of type theory has been studied in several layers: one starts with a definition of “raw” syntax as trees labeled by the names of the generating operations, quotients these trees under permutation of bound variables, and then layers on top of this an additional inductively defined formalism expressing the well-formedness of types, well-formedness of terms, definitional equality of types, and definitional equality of terms. After this, one verifies that definitional equivalence classes of well-formed types and terms can be used as the raw materials to construct a universal model of the type theory that has a universal property: any other model of type theory can be equipped with a unique structure-preserving homomorphism from the universal model. The described universal property determines the universal model up to unique isomorphism, if such a model exists.

We refer to the painstaking process described above as the subjective metatheory, building on the Hegel–Lawvere distinction between objective and subjective approaches to logic. The objective metatheory, in contrast, involves stating and proving results about type theories and programming languages relying only on the universal property of the universal model and not on any specifics of its presentation; the advantage of the objective metatheory is that it is simpler, more direct, more modular, and more composable.

Chapter 1 argues that the subjective metatheory in the sense described is redundant: the decidability conjecture can be stated with respect to any representative of the universal model and does not depend in any way on the raw syntax of type theory, and moreover, for all the type theories considered in this dissertation the existence of at least one representative of the universal model is guaranteed for somewhat trivial reasons that have nothing to do with the specifics of type theory. In this chapter, we develop a logical framework for specifying type theories modularly and working with their universal models in an objective fashion.

Much of this dissertation is stated and proved by exploiting the language of (Grothendieck) topoi, a concept that leads a dual life as a kind of generalized logic and as a kind of generalized topology. The viewpoint of topos theory qua generalized topology plays an important role in this dissertation, and yet it nonetheless remains unfamiliar to most computer scientists and logicians. For this reason, Chapter 2 is provided as sufficient exposition to understand the use of topoi in the remainder of the dissertation, focusing on the recollement or gluing of a topos from a pair of complementary open and closed subtopoi, a classical construction that provides the geometrical basis for synthetic Tait computability.

Universes started their life in the Grothendieck school of algebraic geometry as a technical device to circumvent the annoyance that there cannot be a “set of all sets”; a universe is a set of enough sets, and whilst a universe cannot contain itself, it may nonetheless lie within an even bigger universe. Several important developments in the theory of universes from the 1970s onward by Bénabou, Martin-Löf, Dybjer, Hofmann, Streicher, Awodey, and others have collided to deliver the present-day understanding of the centrality of universes in semantics: a model of type theory is just a special kind of universe in a special kind of category. This chapter provides expository background on the theory of universes, including a novel account of open and closed subuniverses to reflect the recollement theory of topoi from Chapter 2. These open and closed subuniverses will play a critical role in the development of synthetic Tait computability in subsequent chapters.

It is simple enough to verify negative properties of a formal system, e.g. the non-derivability of a given assertion \(\Phi\): find a mathematical object that models all the rules of the formal system and yet refutes \(\Phi\). In contrast, it is much harder to verify any non-trivial positive property of a formal system (such as canonicity, normalization, decidability, etc.). To handle such results, new techniques were needed — and delivered in the late 1960s by Tait, Martin-Löf and others under the name of Tait’s method of computability or logical predicates. Since its inception, Tait’s method has been the primary tool for verifying positive properties of logics, programming languages, and type theories. Early on, Freyd noticed that the logical predicates arguments can be rephrased as model constructions that glue together geometrical objects corresponding to syntax (object) and set theory (meta), setting the stage for this thesis. Thus despite appearances, both positive and negative properties can both be proved using semantic methods.

In the subsequent development of the computability method for applications in computer science, indexed variants of the logical predicates have proved to be fundamental and a number of variations on indexed logical predicates have appeared including the Kripke logical predicates of Jung and Tiuryn and the much more sophisticated Grothendieck logical predicates of Fiore and Simpson as well as Altenkirch, Dybjer, Hofmann, and Scott. This chapter points out that all of these forms of indexing arise in the same way from what is referred to as a figure shape, a continuous map into the classifying space of “Henkin models” of a given theory. Then the (Kripke, Grothendieck, etc.) logical predicates model is presented much more simply as the Artin gluing of this morphism’s inverse image.

An explicit proof of canonicity for the simply typed \(\lambda\)-calculus motivates the abstraction and axiomatization of the geometry of figure shapes and their gluings as a new language for syntactic metatheory, namely synthetic Tait computability. The idea of synthetic Tait computability is to treat both object-level notions (e.g. the collection of terms of type \(\mathsf{bool}\)) and meta-level notions (e.g. a normal form of a given term) in the same language by means of a pair of lex idempotent modalities. One strength of this presentation is that both object-level and meta-level notions can be treated using higher-order abstract syntax (HOAS) in the sense of Hofmann, which greatly simplifies the manipulation of variables.

The first demonstration of the power and modularity of synthetic Tait computability is a new a proof of the canonicity property for Martin-Löf type theory. Unlike traditional proofs of canonicity via non-synthetic Tait computability, the synthetic version is completely modular and broken up into general-purpose lemmas that are stated at a high level of abstraction and can be reused in proofs of different properties for different type theories. (Indeed, some of the constructions isolated in this chapter are used off the shelf in Chapter 7 to prove normalization for cubical type theory.) The modularization of syntactic metatheory is one of the main contributions of this dissertation.

This chapter develops a more sophisticated application of synthetic Tait computability, the proof of normalization and decidability of Martin-Löf’s type theory with a cumulative hierarchy of universes. The synthetic argument contained in this chapter builds on the work of Fiore on categorical normalization by gluing for simply typed λ-calculus, and that of Coquand on a presheaf-theoretic version of normalization by evaluation for dependent types. Analogous to the external argument of Fiore, we construe the syntax of normal and neutral forms as the initial algebra for an internal inductive definition in the language of synthetic Tait computability. The influence of Coquand is visible in the definition of the Tait saturation yoga for dependent types in the synthetic setting, an important closure condition for logical predicates that comprised one of the main innovations of Tait in the context of simply typed combinators. Although this chapter is intended only as “dry run” for the main result (to be exposed in Chapter 7), the normalization argument presented here has intrinsic value: it is the simplest and most direct proof of normalization and decidability for Martin-Löf type theory with \(\eta\)-laws and cumulative universes that has appeared in the literature so far.

This expository chapter introduces cubical type theory as an extension to Martin-Löf’s type theory by an interval \(\mathbb{I}\) with two distinct endpoints \(0, 1 : \mathbb{I}\). The interval is a basic “figure” that defines a notion of path or identification \({u} \mathrel{{\sim} _ {A}} {v}\) between two elements of any type; for instance, to identify \(u,v:A\) is the same as to construct a function \(p: \mathbb{I} \to A\) such that \(p0 = u\) and \(p1=v\). Terms involving variables of type \(\mathbb{I}\) can exhibit complex computational behavior that is difficult to account for: for instance, if \(p: {u} \mathrel{{\sim} _ {A}} {v}\) and \(i: \mathbb{I}\) are variables, then the application \(pi:A\) is a normal form, but it must nonetheless reduce to either \(u\) or \(v\) when \(i\) is substituted for by a constant. Despite appearances, this scenario is fundamentally different from the way that \(pi\) must reduce when \(p\) is replaced by a \(\lambda\)-abstraction, as normal forms must a priori be closed under arbitrary \(\mathbb{I}\)-substitutions — a necessity, because the normal form of an \(n\)-cube must be an \(n\)-cube of normal forms. One of the main technical contributions of this dissertation, introduced in the next chapter, is to generalization of the notion of neutral form and the Tait saturation yoga that smoothly accommodates the problematic computational behavior of the interval.

frontmatter.title: Chapter 7: Normalization for Cubical Type Theory

tree.mainmatter:
This chapter reports the main result of the dissertation, normalization for cubical type theory and its corollaries: injectivity of type constructors, and decidability of equality & typing. These results were first obtained by Sterling and Angiuli for the fragment of cubical type theory without universes; the present chapter extends the results of op. cit. to support a cumulative hierarchy of universes.
The central innovation of this chapter is to generalize the notion of neutral form to accommodate the computational behavior of terms that have free variables of type \(\mathbb{I}\) discussed in our synopsis of Chapter 6. In the conventional account of neutral and normal forms, neutrals \({\color{DarkRed} {e}}\) are built up inductively from \(x\) for term variables \(x : A\), function applications to normal forms \({{\color{DarkRed} {e}}} \mathrel{{\color{DarkRed} {\bullet}}} {{\color{DarkBlue} {m}}}\)and projections from neutral pairs \({{\color{DarkRed} {e}}} {\color{DarkRed} {.1}}\), \({{\color{DarkRed} {e}}} {\color{DarkRed} {.2}}\); our account of neutrals is much the same, except that each neutral form \({\color{DarkRed} {e}}\) comes equipped with a “frontier of instability” \(\boldsymbol{\partial} {{\color{DarkRed} {e}}}\), a predicate on its free \(\mathbb{I}\)-variables that indicates when it “needs to compute further”. We think of a neutral form for an \(n\)-cube as being undefined on its frontier of instability; the process of restricting a neutral to its frontier of instability is then referred to as destabilization.
When \(x : A\) is a variable of an ordinary type, the frontier of instability \(\boldsymbol{\partial} \, {\left ({\color{DarkRed} {\mathsf{var}}} \,{x} \right)}\) is empty because variables never need to compute further. Where something new happens is the path type: given a neutral form \({\color{DarkRed} {e}} : \mathsf{ne} \, {\left ({u} \mathrel{{\sim} _ {A}} {v} \right)}\)of path type, we have for each term \(r : \mathbb{I}\) a neutral form \({{\color{DarkRed} {e}}} \mathrel{{\color{DarkRed} {@}}} {r} : \mathsf{ne} \, A\) whose frontier of instability is defined like so:\[ \boldsymbol{\partial} \, {\left ({{\color{DarkRed} {e}}} \mathrel{{\color{DarkRed} {@}}} {r} \right)} = \boldsymbol{\partial} {\color{DarkRed} {e}} \lor {\left (r=0 \right)} \lor {\left (r=1 \right)}\]
In other words, the path neutral application \({{\color{DarkRed} {e}}} \mathrel{{\color{DarkRed} {@}}} {r}\) needs to compute as soon as e needs to compute, and as soon as the interval term \(r : \mathbb{I}\) becomes equal to a constant. Prior to the introduction of the frontier of instability, the neutrals are embedded into the normals at base types unconditionally, i.e. for each neutral form \({\color{DarkRed} {e}} : \mathsf{ne} \, \mathsf{bool}\), we have a normal form \({\color{DarkBlue} {\lfloor}} {e} {\color{DarkBlue} {\rfloor}} : \mathsf{nf} \, \mathsf{bool}\).
Now that neutrals are equipped with frontiers of instability, a more refined notion of normal form is needed: when \({\color{DarkRed} {e}}\) is a neutral form, the corresponding normal form should contain (recursively) normal forms for \(e\) that are defined under the frontier of instability \(\boldsymbol{\partial} \, {\color{DarkRed} {e}}\). To be more concrete, let \(x : {\mathsf{tt}} \mathrel{{\sim} _ {\mathsf{bool}}} {\mathsf{tt}}\) be a variable of path type and \(r : \mathbb{I}\) is a term; then \({{\color{DarkRed} {\mathsf{var}}} \, x} \mathrel{{\color{DarkRed} {@}}} {r} : \mathsf{ne} \, {\left ({\mathsf{tt}} \mathrel{{\sim} _ {\mathsf{bool}}} {\mathsf{tt}} \right)}\) is a neutral form for the term \(xr\) whose frontier of instability is the boundary \((r = 0) \lor (r = 1)\); the corresponding normal form must therefore glue onto \({{\color{DarkRed} {\mathsf{var}}} \, x} \mathrel{{\color{DarkRed} {@}}} {r}\) additional normal forms for \(x0\) and \(x1\). We refer to the process of completing a neutral with additional data defined on its frontier of instability as stabilization; the stabilized normal form of \(xr\) is then written\[ {\color{DarkBlue} {\lfloor}} {{{\color{DarkRed} {\mathsf{var}}} \, x} \mathrel{{\color{DarkRed} {@}}} {r} \mid r = 0 \hookrightarrow {\color{DarkBlue} {\mathsf{tt}}} , r = 1 \hookrightarrow {\color{DarkBlue} {\mathsf{tt}}}} {\color{DarkBlue} {\rfloor}} \]
where \({\color{DarkBlue} {\mathsf{tt}}}\) is the normal form representing the term \(\mathsf{tt}\)
Just as the embedding of neutrals into normals is “stabilized” by a com patible normal form defined on the neutral’s frontier of instability, so too must the Tait saturation yoga be adjusted. Conventionally one requires the computability predicate for a type \(A\) to be equipped with a function that takes neutral forms \({\color{DarkRed} {e}}\) of terms \(e : A\) to computability witnesses for the same term. In the stabilized Tait saturation yoga, we strengthen the induction hypothesis to require for each neutral form \({\color{DarkRed} {e}}\) a function that extends a computability witness defined only on the frontier of instability \(\boldsymbol{\partial} {\color{DarkRed} {e}}\) to a computability witnessed defined everywhere
The twin innovations of frontiers of instability and stabilization then suffice to adapt the synthetic normalization argument of Chapter 5 to a proof of normalization (and thus decidability) for cubical type theory.

mainmatter.p: This dissertation has focused almost solely on the development and applications of synthetic Tait computability in the context of pure type theory, but the author originally invented synthetic Tait computability to solve problems in core programming languages, as part of Sterling and Harper’s re-analysis of the phase distinction in ML-style module systems between static (compiletime) and dynamic (runtime) code. The purpose of this chapter is to identify several applications of synthetic Tait computability to core programming languages, and set an agenda for future work — some of which has been executed and published following the completion of this dissertation

frontmatter.title: § 8.1: two Phase Distinctions for Program Modules

mainmatter.p: A brief overview is given of the applications of synthetic Tait computability to program modules, material that is published in the Journal of the ACM.

The modal language of synthetic Tait computability promises a new and more abstract account of refinement types and program extraction via a phase distinction between computation and specification. Refinement types are often thought of as a kind of subtype, but there is a fundamental difference: when \({\phi} \sqsubset{A}\) and \({\psi} \sqsubset{B}\) are refinements of types \(A\) and \(B\) respectively, then \(\phi \to \psi\) refines \(A \to B\). In contrast, subtyping laws for function spaces are contravariant in the domain. The refinements available in synthetic Tait computability are moreover proof-relevant in the sense that specification-level code can contain data in addition to properties. The application to proof-relevant refinement types is employed by Niu, Sterling, Grodin, and Harper to develop a logical framework for simultaneously verifying behavior and complexity of functional programs.

Finally, an application of synthetic Tait computability to security and information flow control is identified: a security class is a phase distinction between low and high security. The preliminary results presented in this section have been substantially improved upon by Sterling and Harper, adding support for general recursion and termination-insensitive noninterference by combining synthetic Tait computability with synthetic domain theory.
If \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V}\) is an impredicative pair of universes, then \(\mathcal{U}\) is reflective in \(\mathcal{V}\).

details.p: Our construction of the initial reflection algebra for each \(A: \mathcal{V}\) is one of several equivalent formulations of reflective subuniverse in univalent foundations.

mainmatter.p: We describe some basic definitions and results in “wild (incoherent) category theory”, i.e. the study of untruncated category-like objects in univalent foundations that may lack higher coherences and saturation/completeness assumptions. Some of this is based on the Homotopy Type Theory Coq library, as well as ideas from a blog post of Mike Shulman and private conversations with Mike Shulman.
A wild category is given by the following data:

ol.li: a type \(\mathbf{ob} _ {\mathcal{C}}\);
for each \(A,B: \mathbf{ob} _ {\mathcal{C}}\), a family of types \(\mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{C}} {\left (A , B \right)}\);
for each \(A: \mathbf{ob} _ {\mathcal{C}}\), an element \(1 _ {A} : \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{C}} {\left (A , A \right)}\);

for each \(f: \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{C}} {\left (A , B \right)}\) and \(g: \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{C}} {\left (B , C \right)}\), an element \({\left (f;g \right)} : \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{C}} {\left (A , C \right)}\)

for each \(f: \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{C}} {\left (A , B \right)}\), a identification \(\mathsf{lunit} _ {\mathcal{C}} f : {\left (1 _ {A} ;f \right)} = f\) and a identification \(\mathsf{runit} _ {\mathcal{C}} f : {\left (f; 1 _ {B} \right)} =f\);

for each \(f: \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{C}} {\left (A , B \right)}\) and \(g: \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{C}} {\left (B , C \right)}\) and \(h: \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{C}} {\left (C , D \right)}\), a identification \(\mathsf{assoc} _ {\mathcal{C}} \, f \, g \, h : f; {\left (g;h \right)} = {\left (f;g \right)} ;h\).

mainmatter.p: The definitions here are adapted from Shulman from the setting of intensional type theory to an arbitrary wild category.

Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a wild category; a pre-idempotent on an object \(A: \mathbf{ob} _ {\mathcal{C}}\) is defined to be a morphism \(f: \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{C}} {\left (A , A \right)}\) together with an identification \(\phi : f;f = f\).

tree.mainmatter: Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a wild category; a quasi-idempotent on an object \(A: \mathbf{ob} _ {\mathcal{C}}\) is defined to be a pre-idempotent \({\left (f, \phi \right)}\) on \(A\) together with an identification \(\chi\) between \(f; \phi : f; {\left (f;f \right)} = f;f\) and the following composite identification:\[ f; {\left (f;f \right)} \xrightarrow{\mathsf{assoc} _ {\mathcal{C}} \, f \, f \, f} {\left (f;f \right)} ;f \xrightarrow{\phi ;f} f;f \]

mainmatter.p: Let \(\mathcal{C}\) and \(\mathcal{D}\) be two wild categories. A wild 0-functor from \(\mathcal{C}\) to \(\mathcal{D}\) is given by the following data:

ol.li: a function \(\mathbf{ob} _ {F} : \mathbf{ob} _ {\mathcal{C}} \to \mathbf{ob} _ {\mathcal{D}}\);

for each \(A,B: \mathbf{ob} _ {\mathcal{C}}\), a function \(\mathbf{hom} _ {F} : \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{C}} {\left (A , B \right)} \to \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{D}} {\left (\mathbf{ob} _ {F} A , \mathbf{ob} _ {F} B \right)}\).

mainmatter.p: In other words, a wild 0-functor is a homomorphism of the underlying graphs.

Let \(\mathcal{C}\) and \(\mathcal{D}\) be two wild categories. A wild 1-semi-functor from \(\mathcal{C}\) to \(\mathcal{D}\) is given by a wild 0-functor \(F : \mathcal{C} \to \mathcal{D}\) equipped with, for each \(f: \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{C}} {\left (C , D \right)}\) and \(g : \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{C}} {\left (C , D \right)}\), an identification \(\mathsf{seq} _F \, f \, g : \mathbf{hom} _ {F} {\left (f;g \right)} = \mathbf{hom} _ {F} {f} ; \mathbf{hom} _ {G} {g} \).

tree.mainmatter:
Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a wild category; the identity 0-functor \({\mathcal{C}} \xrightarrow{1 _ {\mathcal{C}}} {\mathcal{C}}\) on \(\mathcal{C}\) is defined as follows:\[\begin{aligned} \mathbf{ob} _ {1 _ {\mathcal{C}}} A & : \equiv A \\ \mathbf{hom} _ {1 _ {\mathcal{C}}} f & : \equiv f \end{aligned}\]
The above may be extended to a wild 1-semi-functor as follows:\[ \mathsf{seq} _ {F} \, f \, g : \equiv \mathsf{refl}\]

Let \(\mathcal{C}\) and \(\mathcal{D}\) be wild categories, and let \(D: \mathbf{ob} _ {\mathcal{D}}\) be an object of \(\mathcal{D}\). We define the constant 0-functor on \(D: \mathbf{ob} _ {\mathcal{D}}\) out of \(\mathcal{C}\) to be the 0-functor \({\mathcal{C}} \xrightarrow{\Delta D} {\mathcal{D}}\) defined as follows:\[\begin{aligned} \mathbf{ob} _ {\Delta D} C & : \equiv D \\ \mathbf{hom} _ {\Delta D} f & : \equiv 1 _ {D} \end{aligned}\]
The above may be extended to a wild 1-semi-functor as follows:\[\mathsf{seq} _ {F} \, f \, g : \equiv \mathsf{refl}\]

mainmatter.p: Let \(\mathcal{C} , \mathcal{D}\) be two wild categories, and let \(F,G: \mathcal{C} \to \mathcal{D}\) be two wild 0-functors from \(\mathcal{C}\) to \(\mathcal{D}\). A wild natural transformation is defined by the following data:

ol.li: for each \(A: \mathbf{ob} _ {\mathcal{C}}\), an element \(\alpha _ {A} : \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{D}} {\left (\mathbf{ob} _ {F} A , \mathbf{ob} _ {G} A \right)}\);
for each \(f: \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{C}} {\left (A , B \right)}\), an identification \(\alpha _ {f} : \alpha _A; \mathbf{hom} _ {G} {f} = \mathbf{hom} _ {F} {f} ; \alpha _B\).

mainmatter.p: Let \(\mathcal{C} , \mathcal{D}\) be two wild categories, and let \(F,G: \mathcal{C} \to \mathcal{D}\) be two wild 1-semi-functors from \(\mathcal{C}\) to \(\mathcal{D}\). A wild semi-coherent natural transformation is defined by the data of a wild natural transformation \(\alpha : F \to G\) together with, for each \(f: \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{C}} {\left (A , B \right)}\) and \(g : \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{C}} {\left (B , C \right)}\), a higher identification \(\mathsf{seq} _ {\alpha} \, f \, g\) between \(\alpha _ {f;g} : F {\left (f;g \right)} ; \alpha _ {C} = \alpha _ {A} ;G {\left (f;g \right)}\) and the composite identification:

frontmatter.title: Wild Initial Objects From Universal Cones

mainmatter.p: Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a wild category, and let \(I: \mathbf{ob} _ {\mathcal{C}}\) be an object of \(\mathcal{C}\). If we may equip a wild natural transformation \({\Delta I} \xrightarrow{\alpha} {1 _ {\mathcal{C}}}\) from the constant 0-functor \({\mathcal{C}} \xrightarrow{\Delta I} {\mathcal{C}}\) to the identity 0-functor \({\mathcal{C}} \xrightarrow{1 _ {\mathcal{C}}} {\mathcal{C}}\) with an identification \(c_ \alpha : \alpha _I = 1 _ {I}\), then \(\mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{C}} {\left (I , A \right)}\) is contractible for all \(A: \mathbf{ob} _ {\mathcal{C}}\).

(Our proof will follow that of the [nLab](https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/initial+object#cone), which was stated for ordinary categories and functors and natural transformations rather than wild ones. It was Mike Shulman who noticed who noticed that this proof also works for the wild/incoherent notions studied here.)

details.p: To show that \(\mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{C}} {\left (I , A \right)}\) is contractible, we choose \(\alpha _ {A} : \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{C}} {\left (I , A \right)}\) as the center of contraction. To identify any \(f : \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{C}} {\left (I , A \right)}\) with \(\alpha _ {A}\), we use the wild naturality of \({\Delta I} \xrightarrow{\alpha} {1 _ {\mathcal{C}}}\). In particular, we have an identification \(\alpha _f\) between \(\alpha _I;f\) and \(1 _ {I} ; \alpha _A\). By our assumption that \(\alpha _I= 1 _ {I}\) and using the wild left-unit identifications \(\lambda f\) and \(\lambda \alpha _A\), we may identify \(f\) with \(\alpha _A\).

frontmatter.title: A Wild Universal Cone From a Splitting of the Generic Element

mainmatter.p: Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a wild category, and let \(I: \mathbf{ob} _ {\mathcal{C}}\) be an object of \(\mathcal{C}\) such that \({\Delta I} \xrightarrow{\alpha} {1 _ {\mathcal{C}}}\) is a wild natural transformation from the constant 0-functor \({\mathcal{C}} \xrightarrow{\Delta I} {\mathcal{C}}\) to the identity 0-functor \({\mathcal{C}} \xrightarrow{1 _ {\mathcal{C}}} {\mathcal{C}}\) on \(\mathcal{C}\) for some \(I: \mathbf{ob} _ {\mathcal{C}}\). Now let \(r: \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{C}} {\left (I , J \right)}\) and \(s: \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{C}} {\left (J , I \right)}\) be a section-retraction pair witnessed by \(\eta : s;r = 1 _ {I}\), and let \(\sigma : r;s= \alpha _ {I}\) be a splitting of \(\alpha _ {I} : \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{C}} {\left (I , I \right)}\). Then we may define a wild natural transformation \({\Delta J} \xrightarrow{\beta} {1 _ {\mathcal{C}}}\) and an identification \(\chi : \beta _ {J} = 1 _ {J}\).
(This construction is due to Mike Shulman.)

section.details:
Proof.
Define the component \(\beta _ {A} : \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{C}} {\left (J , A \right)}\) to be the composite \(s; \alpha _ {A}\); for the wild naturality structure, we fix \(f: \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{C}} {\left (A , B \right)}\) to define \(\beta _ {f} : \beta _ {A} ;f = 1 _ {J} ; \beta _ {B}\) as follows: \[\begin{aligned} \beta _ {A} ;f & = {\left (s; \alpha _ {A} \right)} ;f \\ & = s; {\left (1 _ {A} ; \alpha _ {B} \right)} \\ & = s; \alpha _ {B} \\ & = 1 _ {J} ; {\left (s; \alpha _ {B} \right)} \\ & = 1 _ {J} ; \beta _ {B} \end{aligned} \]
Next we identify \(\beta _ {J}\) with \(1 _ {J}\). By incoherent naturality, we have \(\alpha _ {J} ;s = \alpha _ {I}\); thus by our splitting of \(\alpha _ {I}\) we have \(\alpha _ {J} ;s = r;s\) and hence by postcomposing \(r\) and canceling via \(\eta :s;r= 1 _ {I}\) we conclude that \(\alpha _J = r\). Therefore \(\beta _J=s; \alpha _J=s;r= 1 _ {J}\).

frontmatter.title: Idempotents in Intensional Type Theory
frontmatter.meta: @article{shulman-2016, author = {Shulman, Michael}, year = {2016}, month = apr, doi = {10.2168/LMCS-12(3:9)2016}, issue = {3}, journal = {Logical Methods in Computer Science}, title = {{Idempotents in intensional type theory}}, volume = {12}, }

mainmatter.p: We study idempotents in intensional Martin-Löf type theory, and in particular the question of when and whether they split. We show that in the presence of propositional truncation and Voevodsky’s univalence axiom, there exist idempotents that do not split; thus in plain MLTT not all idempotents can be proven to split. On the other hand, assuming only function extensionality, an idempotent can be split if and only if its witness of idempotency satisfies one extra coherence condition. Both proofs are inspired by parallel results of Lurie in higher category theory, showing that ideas from higher category theory and homotopy theory can have applications even in ordinary MLTT.

Finally, we show that although the witness of idempotency can be recovered from a splitting, the one extra coherence condition cannot in general; and we construct “the type of fully coherent idempotents”, by splitting an idempotent on the type of partially coherent ones. Our results have been formally verified in the proof assistant Coq.

frontmatter.title: Impredicative Encodings of (Higher) Inductive Types
frontmatter.meta: @inproceedings{awodey-frey-speight-2018, author = {Awodey, Steve and Frey, Jonas and Speight, Sam}, address = {Oxford, United Kingdom}, publisher = {Association for Computing Machinery}, booktitle = {Proceedings of the 33rd Annual ACM/IEEE Symposium on Logic in Computer Science}, year = {2018}, doi = {10.1145/3209108.3209130}, isbn = {978-1-4503-5583-4}, pages = {76--85}, title = {Impredicative Encodings of (Higher) Inductive Types}, }

mainmatter.p: Postulating an impredicative universe in dependent type theory allows System F style encodings of finitary inductive types, but these fail to satisfy the relevant η-equalities and consequently do not admit dependent eliminators. To recover η and dependent elimination, we present a method to construct refinements of these impredicative encodings, using ideas from homotopy type theory. We then extend our method to construct impredicative encodings of some higher inductive types, such as 1-truncation and the unit circle S1.
